sections.title: Logical Reasoning
sections.directions: Directions: Read each short passage and choose the answer that best responds to the question. Do not assume anything beyond what is stated unless the question asks for an assumption.

questions.stimulus: After Riverton installed speed cameras on three avenues, reported collisions on those avenues fell by 18 percent. The transportation commissioner concluded that the cameras caused the reduction and should therefore be installed on every major road in the city.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the commissioner's reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It treats a decline in reported collisions as evidence about unreported collisions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It assumes that a measure effective on some roads must be equally useful on all major roads.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It overlooks the possibility that drivers generally dislike speed cameras.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It mistakes a citywide trend for a trend limited to three avenues.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It concludes that collisions declined before establishing that speeding was ever illegal.

questions.explanation: The evidence concerns three avenues only. The recommendation extends the same policy to every major road without showing that the conditions are comparable.

questions.stimulus: A museum replaced long explanatory plaques with shorter labels and interactive audio. Average time spent in the featured gallery rose by 25 percent, and exit surveys showed more visitors could name at least one artist from the exhibit. The curator concludes that the new format improved visitor engagement.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the curator's argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The featured gallery was located farther from the museum cafe than most other galleries.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Visitors who spent more time in the gallery before the change were usually art students.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The museum did not change its advertising, admission price, or exhibit topic during the comparison period.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Some visitors prefer reading labels to listening to audio.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Several other museums have recently tested interactive audio systems.

questions.explanation: If other major variables stayed the same, the change in labels and audio is a more plausible cause of the improved engagement measures.

questions.stimulus: The city arborist argues that Brookvale should replace dying maple trees along sidewalks with a drought-resistant species. The drought-resistant species needs less watering, and Brookvale has imposed strict limits on municipal water use during summers.
questions.stem: The arborist's argument depends on assuming which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Residents prefer the appearance of drought-resistant trees to the appearance of maple trees.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The drought-resistant species can thrive in the other conditions present along Brookvale's sidewalks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Brookvale's water limits apply only to trees planted on public land.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Most of the dying maple trees were planted less than ten years ago.

choices.label: E
choices.text: No city outside Brookvale has planted the drought-resistant species.

questions.explanation: The recommendation requires that the replacement species be suitable for more than drought conditions, including the actual sidewalk environment.

questions.stimulus: A national report says telework has not changed worker productivity because a survey of large technology companies found no change in output per employee. The report concludes that telework policies are irrelevant to productivity in the economy as a whole.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the strongest criticism of the report's reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It relies on productivity rather than worker satisfaction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It treats evidence from one sector and company size as representative of the entire economy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It assumes that employees prefer telework to office work.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It fails to specify whether telework is full-time or part-time.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It concludes only that telework is irrelevant, not that telework is harmful.

questions.explanation: Large technology companies are too narrow a sample to support a conclusion about the economy as a whole.

questions.stimulus: A messaging app advertises that it protects privacy because messages are encrypted. However, the app keeps an unencrypted copy of each user's contact list on company servers. Therefore, the app's privacy claim is misleading.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most users of messaging apps do not read privacy policies carefully.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The app's advertisements specifically define its privacy claim as applying only to message content, not contact lists.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some competing apps also store contact lists on company servers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encryption can be implemented in several technically different ways.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The company deletes inactive accounts after five years.

questions.explanation: If the claim is expressly limited to message content, the contact-list practice does not show that the advertised claim is misleading.

questions.stimulus: Attorney: A witness who is confident is not necessarily accurate. Psychological studies show that confidence can be inflated by repeated questioning. Jurors should therefore be warned not to treat confidence alone as proof of accuracy.
questions.stem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps justify the attorney's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A legal rule should never be adopted unless it benefits defendants and prosecutors equally.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Jurors should be cautioned whenever a commonly persuasive feature of testimony can be produced by something other than accuracy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Witness testimony should be excluded whenever any psychological study raises doubts about it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Jurors should rely only on physical evidence when deciding whether testimony is true.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Courts should use warnings only for evidence that is certain to be false.

questions.explanation: The principle connects the evidence about confidence being caused by repeated questioning to the need for a jury warning.

questions.stimulus: A cereal is advertised as a healthy breakfast because it contains several vitamins. Yet a serving contains more sugar than a serving of many desserts. So the cereal cannot be a healthy breakfast.
questions.stem: The reasoning in the argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it

choices.label: A
choices.text: rejects a health claim merely because one unhealthy feature is present, without considering the overall nutritional profile.

choices.label: B
choices.text: assumes that vitamins are never beneficial in breakfast foods.

choices.label: C
choices.text: infers that all desserts are healthier than cereals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: relies on an appeal to the popularity of sugary foods.

choices.label: E
choices.text: confuses the amount of sugar in a serving with the total number of servings eaten.

questions.explanation: The argument jumps from one negative trait to an absolute conclusion about healthfulness.

questions.stimulus: The council will fund the river greenway only if the project will increase public access to the river. The project will increase public access if at least two new pedestrian bridges are included. The current proposal includes three new pedestrian bridges.
questions.stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The council will fund the river greenway.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The river greenway will increase public access to the river.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any project with pedestrian bridges should be funded.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The council funds only projects that include three pedestrian bridges.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The current proposal is the only way to increase public access to the river.

questions.explanation: Three bridges satisfy the sufficient condition for increasing access. Funding does not follow because access is only necessary for funding, not sufficient.

questions.stimulus: Coins bearing the image of Queen Alia have been found in a coastal settlement. The coins were minted between 410 and 420 CE. Therefore, the settlement must have been occupied at some point after 410 CE.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is most strongly supported by the information above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The settlement was founded after 410 CE.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Queen Alia personally visited the coastal settlement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The settlement was occupied at least as late as the earliest possible minting date of the coins.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coins were the only items traded in the settlement after 410 CE.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The settlement was abandoned before 420 CE.

questions.explanation: The coins could not have been present before they were minted, so the settlement was occupied no earlier than 410 CE.

questions.stimulus: A manufacturer claims that outsourcing customer service will save money because the contractor charges less per call than the manufacturer's current in-house department. The manufacturer has not considered whether outsourced calls will take longer or require more follow-up contacts.
questions.stem: Which one of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating the claim?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether the contractor's employees work in the same time zone as the manufacturer's headquarters.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether the total cost per resolved customer issue would be lower under the contractor's system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether customers prefer phone service to online chat service.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether the current department has ever won an internal award.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether the contractor has more employees than the manufacturer does.

questions.explanation: Savings depend on the total cost of resolving issues, not merely on the cost of each individual call.

questions.stimulus: A new drug caused nausea in 3 percent of trial participants. Since the trial included 4,000 participants, the drug is unsafe for general use.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The trial participants ranged in age from 20 to 80.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The drug treats a condition that, without treatment, often causes severe organ damage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Many trial participants had taken other medications before the study began.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The drug was tested at several research hospitals.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nausea was reported more often by participants who had read the list of possible side effects.

questions.explanation: A modest side effect may not make a drug unsafe if the untreated condition is far more serious.

questions.stimulus: Critic: A novel is original only if it does something no earlier novel has done. The novel Lake of Ashes is original because no earlier novel has combined courtroom testimony with recipes in alternating chapters.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the critic's argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some readers find recipes distracting when included in novels.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Many courtroom novels have been published during the last century.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No earlier novel used the same alternating structure of testimony and recipes that Lake of Ashes uses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The author of Lake of Ashes is a trained chef.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Several critics disliked the ending of Lake of Ashes.

questions.explanation: The critic's standard for originality is novelty, and choice C supports the claim that the relevant feature is novel.

questions.stimulus: A contract states that a tenant may terminate the lease early only if the landlord receives written notice at least 60 days before the termination date. Mara emailed notice 70 days before the desired date, and the landlord acknowledges receiving the email that day. The lease defines email as written notice.
questions.stem: Which one of the following follows logically from the statements above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mara complied with the notice requirement for early termination.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mara must pay rent for at least 70 more days.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The landlord may reject all notices sent by email.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The lease can never be terminated early for any other reason.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mara's termination is valid only if the landlord agrees to it in writing.

questions.explanation: The stated conditions were met: written notice by email, received at least 60 days before termination.

questions.stimulus: An environmental group argues that the city must either ban all coastal construction or accept the eventual loss of its wetlands. Since banning all coastal construction is economically impossible, the city should accept that its wetlands will be lost.
questions.stem: The argument's reasoning is most vulnerable to which one of the following criticisms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It offers no evidence that wetlands are valuable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It presents two possibilities as exhaustive without considering partial restrictions or mitigation measures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It assumes that economic impossibility is the same as legal impossibility.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It relies on public opinion rather than scientific evidence.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It treats the loss of wetlands as a benefit rather than a cost.

questions.explanation: The reasoning depends on a false choice between a total ban and total loss.

questions.stimulus: At Northgate High, students who used an online tutoring platform improved their math scores more than students who did not. Yet teachers report that the platform's explanations are often confusing. Both statements can be true because most platform users also attended optional after-school workshops.
questions.stem: The final sentence of the passage functions primarily to

choices.label: A
choices.text: explain how the observed score improvement could occur despite a weakness in the platform.

choices.label: B
choices.text: show that the platform's explanations are actually clear.

choices.label: C
choices.text: prove that the workshops were required for all students.

choices.label: D
choices.text: establish that teachers were mistaken about the platform.

choices.label: E
choices.text: argue that math scores are not a valid measure of learning.

questions.explanation: The additional workshop information reconciles the improvement data with the report that the platform itself can be confusing.

questions.stimulus: Restaurant inspectors found violations in 40 percent of inspected restaurants last year. This year they found violations in 25 percent. Therefore, restaurants are following health rules better this year.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: This year inspectors focused on restaurants with excellent prior inspection records.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Most restaurants inspected this year served lunch as well as dinner.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Restaurant inspectors receive the same training each year.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The total number of restaurants in the city has increased slightly.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some violations are easier to correct than others.

questions.explanation: If this year's sample was biased toward previously compliant restaurants, the lower violation rate may not reflect overall improvement.

questions.stimulus: A streaming service pays independent musicians more per play than other major services do. Therefore, musicians who want to maximize income should release music exclusively through that service.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Independent musicians always prefer income to artistic control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The higher payment per play would not be outweighed by receiving fewer total plays on that service.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Other major services never promote independent musicians.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Listeners can subscribe to only one streaming service at a time.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Exclusive releases are illegal unless musicians own their recordings.

questions.explanation: Income depends on payment per play multiplied by number of plays. A higher rate alone does not guarantee higher total income.

questions.stimulus: Public health official: When a vaccine substantially reduces the risk of transmitting a dangerous disease, institutions that serve vulnerable populations may require it for staff, provided medical exemptions are available.
questions.stem: Which one of the following judgments most closely conforms to the official's principle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A nursing home may require staff to receive a vaccine that reduces transmission of a severe illness, while allowing exemptions for staff who cannot safely receive it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A bookstore may require customers to receive any vaccine that its owner personally prefers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A hospital may fire staff for refusing a vaccine that has no effect on transmission.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A school may require a vaccine but deny all medical exemptions to avoid paperwork.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A restaurant may require staff vaccination solely because the vaccine is inexpensive.

questions.explanation: Choice A matches all elements: vulnerable population, reduced transmission of a dangerous disease, staff requirement, and medical exemptions.

questions.stimulus: Urban planner: The downtown bike lanes did not cause the recent increase in store vacancies. Vacancies rose at the same rate in neighborhoods without bike lanes, and online sales increased sharply throughout the city during the same period.
questions.stem: The main conclusion of the urban planner's argument is that

choices.label: A
choices.text: online sales increased sharply throughout the city.

choices.label: B
choices.text: neighborhoods without bike lanes experienced rising vacancies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the downtown bike lanes were not responsible for the increase in store vacancies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: bike lanes should be installed in neighborhoods without them.

choices.label: E
choices.text: downtown stores are less profitable than online retailers.

questions.explanation: The other statements are offered as support for the planner's denial of a causal claim.

questions.stimulus: A translator says that a poem's rhyme scheme cannot be preserved in translation without sacrificing literal meaning. A new translation preserves the poem's rhyme scheme. Therefore, the new translation must sacrifice literal meaning.
questions.stem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The new translation is the first translation of the poem.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Any translation that preserves the rhyme scheme of this poem sacrifices literal meaning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Literal meaning matters more than rhyme scheme in every poem.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The original poem's rhyme scheme is unusually complex.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Readers cannot detect rhyme schemes in translated poems.

questions.explanation: The assumed conditional directly links preserving this poem's rhyme scheme to sacrificing literal meaning.

questions.stimulus: A startup's revenue doubled this year, so its investors concluded that the company is now profitable. But the company also doubled its staff and leased a larger facility.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the investors' conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The startup's expenses increased by only 20 percent during the same year.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The startup has a memorable logo.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Several competitors also doubled their revenue this year.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The larger facility is located in a fashionable district.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The startup plans to hire more employees next year.

questions.explanation: Profitability depends on revenue exceeding expenses. If revenue doubled while expenses rose only 20 percent, profitability becomes more likely.

questions.stimulus: Judge: Evidence obtained by a drone flying over a fenced backyard should be excluded because people reasonably expect privacy in fenced backyards. The drone did not physically enter the yard, but physical entry is not the only way to violate privacy.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best expresses the role played by the statement that the drone did not physically enter the yard?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is the main conclusion of the argument.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a fact acknowledged by the judge that could be thought to count against the conclusion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is presented as the only evidence supporting exclusion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is used to show that fenced backyards lack privacy expectations.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is an example of a rule the judge wants to adopt.

questions.explanation: The judge concedes a point that might weaken exclusion, then argues that it does not defeat the privacy claim.

questions.stimulus: Ravens hide food when other ravens can see them, but later move the food if they were watched. Researchers infer that ravens can track what another raven has observed.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, would most support the researchers' inference?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ravens sometimes hide food in more than one location.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ravens moved food only when the observer raven had a clear line of sight during the original hiding.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ravens prefer some foods to others.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Other bird species also hide food.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The researchers used cameras to record the ravens.

questions.explanation: The behavior varies with what the observer could actually see, supporting the idea that ravens track another's observation.

questions.stimulus: A historian found two maps of the same harbor. The first shows a lighthouse but no railway. The second shows the railway but labels the lighthouse 'ruins.' City records show the railway opened in 1888, and the lighthouse collapsed in 1894.
questions.stem: Which one of the following must be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The first map was drawn before 1888.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The second map was drawn after 1894.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The first map was drawn after the second map.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both maps were drawn by the same cartographer.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The railway caused the lighthouse to collapse.

questions.explanation: A map that labels the lighthouse as ruins must have been drawn after the lighthouse collapsed in 1894.

questions.stimulus: Software tester: Every program that passes our security audit has no known critical vulnerability. This program has no known critical vulnerability. Therefore, it passed our security audit.
questions.stem: The pattern of reasoning is most similar to which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every certified translator knows two languages. Mira knows two languages. Therefore, Mira is a certified translator.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every valid ticket has a barcode. This ticket lacks a barcode. Therefore, this ticket is not valid.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every ripe apple is sweet. This apple is ripe. Therefore, it is sweet.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every closed shop has locked doors. This shop is not closed. Therefore, its doors are not locked.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Every city museum is public. This museum is not public. Therefore, it is not a city museum.

questions.explanation: Both arguments affirm the necessary condition: if audited then no known vulnerability; no known vulnerability, so audited.

questions.stimulus: A farm's wheat yield increased after the farmer added a soil microbe mixture. The farmer concludes that the mixture caused the increase. Rainfall was also unusually favorable that year.
questions.stem: Which one of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating the farmer's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether nearby farms with similar rainfall but no microbe mixture had comparable yield increases.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether the farmer prefers wheat to other crops.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the microbe mixture came in recyclable packaging.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether the farm has been owned by the same family for decades.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether wheat prices rose that year.

questions.explanation: A comparison group helps separate the effect of the mixture from the effect of unusually favorable rainfall.

sections.title: Reading Comprehension
sections.directions: Directions: Read each passage or pair of passages and answer the questions that follow. Base your answers on what is stated or strongly supported by the passage.

passages.title: Algorithmic Rulemaking and Administrative Law
passages.text:
Administrative agencies increasingly use algorithmic tools to screen permits, detect fraud, and prioritize enforcement. Some scholars argue that these tools threaten administrative law's traditional demand for reasoned decision-making. A rule may be lawful only if an agency explains the facts and policy judgments that support it. When an agency relies on a proprietary model, however, the model's internal logic may be unavailable to the public and even to agency officials. Critics therefore worry that algorithmic governance will replace public reasons with technical opacity.

Defenders respond that this worry is overstated. Agencies have long relied on complex technical methods, including statistical risk models, engineering formulas, and actuarial tables. The legal system has rarely required every citizen to understand every calculation. Instead, courts usually ask whether the agency identified the relevant factors, considered important objections, and used a method reasonably connected to the statutory goal. On this view, algorithmic tools should be judged by familiar standards, not treated as uniquely suspect.

A more careful position lies between these two views. Algorithms are not magical black boxes, but neither are they ordinary spreadsheets. Their distinctive problem is that they can distribute responsibility. A vendor builds a model, agency staff choose training data, supervisors set thresholds, and frontline officials apply the output. If no one can explain how these choices interact, the public cannot know whom to challenge or what to contest. The demand for reasoned decision-making should therefore focus less on exposing every line of code and more on documenting the human choices that shape the system's design, use, and revision.

passages.title: Fungal Networks and Drought Resilience
passages.text:
For decades, plant biologists treated mycorrhizal fungi mainly as nutrient brokers. The fungi colonize plant roots, receive sugars produced by photosynthesis, and in return help plants acquire phosphorus and other minerals from soil. Recent research has complicated that picture. In dry regions, some fungal partners appear to improve drought tolerance by changing the plant's water-use behavior, not simply by increasing mineral uptake.

One study of desert shrubs found that plants associated with a particular fungal strain kept their leaf pores, or stomata, partly closed during the hottest hours of the day. This reduced water loss, although it also limited photosynthesis. The plants grew more slowly in wet years but survived drought years at higher rates than shrubs lacking that strain. The finding challenged the assumption that beneficial symbiosis must always increase immediate growth. A partnership may be beneficial because it shifts a plant toward caution rather than speed.

The practical implications are tempting but uncertain. Agricultural companies have begun marketing fungal inoculants that promise drought-ready crops. Yet a strain that helps a shrub in desert soil may fail in a field where irrigation, fertilizer, and crop genetics create different incentives. Symbiosis is not a plug-in accessory. It is a negotiation shaped by environment, plant species, and microbial competitors. The new research is valuable not because it yields a universal fungal solution, but because it broadens the questions scientists ask about resilience.

passages.title: Restoring Music for Silent Film
passages.text:
Silent films were rarely silent. In large theaters, orchestras performed compiled scores that mixed popular songs, classical excerpts, and original themes. In small towns, a single pianist might improvise from cue sheets suggesting moods such as 'agitated' or 'pastoral.' Because the music was live, a film's sound could vary dramatically from one venue to another. Modern restorers therefore face a puzzle: what does it mean to restore the music of a silent film?

One approach seeks historical reconstruction. If archives contain a conductor's score used at a premiere, restorers may record it and pair it with the restored image. This method offers a valuable glimpse of how at least one audience may have experienced the film. But reconstruction can create a false sense of finality. A premiere score was not necessarily the definitive score, especially for films distributed widely with different musical instructions.

A second approach commissions new music that responds to the film for contemporary audiences. Purists sometimes object that new scores overwrite history. Yet new music can capture an important historical truth: the film was designed to be completed in performance. The danger is not newness itself, but music that draws attention away from the film's visual rhythm. The most responsible restorations, whether historical or newly commissioned, treat music as an interpretive partner rather than as a museum label attached to the image.

passages.title: Comparative Reading - Vacant Lots and Urban Value
passages.text:
Passage A
Urban vacant lots are often described as failures of planning: parcels waiting to be converted into housing, offices, or taxable commercial space. But in many neighborhoods, residents already use these lots as informal commons. They plant gardens, hold block parties, repair bicycles, and create paths between streets. These uses may not appear on property ledgers, yet they produce social value. A policy that treats every vacant lot as idle land risks erasing local networks that city officials did not create and may not notice. Cities should therefore recognize community stewardship as a legitimate form of urban value, even when it produces little immediate revenue.

Passage B
Vacant lots can host community activities, but cities also face severe housing shortages and shrinking tax bases. Romanticizing informal use may preserve scarcity for those who already live nearby while excluding people who need homes. Development need not erase community value; zoning agreements can require affordable units, public courtyards, and space for neighborhood groups. The harder question is not whether vacant land has social meaning, but whether that meaning justifies blocking development that could serve a broader public. Urban policy should convert underused land into inclusive, regulated projects rather than freeze it in informal arrangements.

questions.stem: The passage is primarily concerned with

choices.label: A
choices.text: arguing that courts should ban the use of proprietary software by agencies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: comparing two extreme views about algorithmic agency action and proposing a middle approach.

choices.label: C
choices.text: describing the technical architecture of modern machine-learning systems.

choices.label: D
choices.text: showing that administrative agencies have never provided adequate public reasons.

choices.label: E
choices.text: defending vendors from criticism by administrative law scholars.

questions.explanation: The passage presents critics, defenders, and then a compromise focused on documenting human choices.

questions.stem: According to the passage, critics of algorithmic governance are most concerned that agencies will

choices.label: A
choices.text: be forced to publish too much confidential citizen data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: use tools whose decision-making logic cannot be meaningfully examined by the public.

choices.label: C
choices.text: stop using statistical methods that courts have already approved.

choices.label: D
choices.text: allow judges to make policy choices reserved for agencies.

choices.label: E
choices.text: spend excessive funds training staff to write computer code.

questions.explanation: The first paragraph identifies opacity and lack of public reasons as the core concern.

questions.stem: The author mentions engineering formulas and actuarial tables primarily to

choices.label: A
choices.text: show that agencies have previously relied on complex technical methods.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prove that algorithmic tools are never more complicated than older methods.

choices.label: C
choices.text: argue that courts should learn advanced mathematics.

choices.label: D
choices.text: suggest that actuarial tables are unconstitutional.

choices.label: E
choices.text: contrast public agencies with private insurance companies.

questions.explanation: These examples support defenders' claim that technical complexity is not new in administrative law.

questions.stem: Which one of the following best states the author's view of the legal demand for reasoned decision-making in algorithmic contexts?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It should require publication of every line of source code.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It should be abandoned because algorithms cannot be explained.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It should emphasize records of human design, data, threshold, and revision choices.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It should apply only when a citizen can prove the model made an error.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It should defer entirely to software vendors.

questions.explanation: The final sentence states the author's proposed focus.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that one distinctive feature of algorithmic tools is that they

choices.label: A
choices.text: always make decisions more accurate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: can make accountability harder by spreading choices across several actors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: are illegal whenever they are proprietary.

choices.label: D
choices.text: require no human involvement after purchase.

choices.label: E
choices.text: are easier for the public to understand than traditional agency rules.

questions.explanation: The third paragraph says algorithms can distribute responsibility among vendors and agency actors.

questions.stem: Which one of the following would most clearly satisfy the author's proposed approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An agency refuses to disclose anything about a model because a vendor owns it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An agency publishes the model's source code but keeps secret the data-selection choices.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An agency records why it chose particular training data, enforcement thresholds, and review procedures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An agency promises that the model is accurate but refuses to explain how it is used.

choices.label: E
choices.text: An agency lets the vendor revise the model without telling agency staff.

questions.explanation: This choice documents the human choices the author says should be made visible.

questions.stem: The main point of the passage is that recent research on mycorrhizal fungi

choices.label: A
choices.text: has shown that all crops should be treated with fungal inoculants.

choices.label: B
choices.text: has replaced the nutrient-broker model with a simple drought-proofing model.

choices.label: C
choices.text: has broadened understanding of fungal benefits while cautioning against universal applications.

choices.label: D
choices.text: has proved that plant growth rates are never important to survival.

choices.label: E
choices.text: has shown that fungi harm plants in wet years.

questions.explanation: The passage broadens the role of fungi and ends with caution about practical application.

questions.stem: The desert shrub study suggests that a symbiotic relationship can be beneficial even when it

choices.label: A
choices.text: reduces short-term growth under some conditions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prevents plants from photosynthesizing at all.

choices.label: C
choices.text: eliminates the need for water.

choices.label: D
choices.text: works only by increasing phosphorus uptake.

choices.label: E
choices.text: requires plants to abandon root systems.

questions.explanation: The shrubs grew more slowly in wet years but survived drought at higher rates.

questions.stem: In the context of the passage, 'caution rather than speed' most nearly refers to

choices.label: A
choices.text: a plant strategy that favors water conservation over rapid growth.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a scientist's reluctance to publish surprising results.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a company's decision to delay marketing a product.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a fungus's preference for slow reproduction.

choices.label: E
choices.text: a farmer's refusal to irrigate crops.

questions.explanation: The phrase describes partly closing stomata, conserving water but limiting photosynthesis.

questions.stem: The author would most likely agree with which one of the following statements about commercial fungal inoculants?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They should be assumed to work for any crop grown in a dry place.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Their effectiveness depends on context and should not be inferred from one successful strain alone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are useless because fungi cannot affect plant water use.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They work only when fertilizer is absent from the soil.

choices.label: E
choices.text: They should be evaluated solely by wet-year growth rates.

questions.explanation: The final paragraph stresses uncertainty and context dependence.

questions.stem: The passage indicates that the older view of mycorrhizal fungi emphasized

choices.label: A
choices.text: the fungi's role in helping plants obtain soil nutrients.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the fungi's ability to control plant genetics.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the fungi's competition with desert shrubs for sunlight.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the fungi's use as a universal agricultural product.

choices.label: E
choices.text: the fungi's tendency to close plant stomata.

questions.explanation: The first paragraph describes the older model as nutrient brokerage.

questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes the function of the final sentence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It dismisses the scientific value of the research.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It identifies the value of the research while limiting exaggerated practical claims.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It proves that agricultural companies are intentionally dishonest.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It summarizes the chemical process of photosynthesis.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It introduces a new study not discussed earlier.

questions.explanation: The final sentence says the value is in broadening questions, not in a universal solution.

questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, would most directly support the author's caution about applying the shrub study to crops?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The same fungal strain improved drought survival in shrubs but had no effect on a wheat variety grown in fertilized soil.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some farmers prefer crops that mature early.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Desert shrubs are found in many regions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Photosynthesis produces sugars used by plants.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Many fungi can be seen only with a microscope.

questions.explanation: This choice shows that effectiveness can vary with plant and environment, exactly the author's caution.

questions.stem: Which one of the following best states the author's main point?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Silent films should be shown without music to avoid historical distortion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A premiere score is always the most historically accurate music for a silent film.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Restoring silent film music requires recognizing both historical evidence and the interpretive nature of performance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: New scores are preferable because contemporary audiences dislike older music.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cue sheets are more reliable than conductor's scores.

questions.explanation: The author weighs reconstruction and new scores, then argues for music as interpretive partnership.

questions.stem: The passage states that in small towns, silent-film music was often

choices.label: A
choices.text: performed by a single pianist using mood cues.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prohibited by theater owners.

choices.label: C
choices.text: recorded in advance by the film studio.

choices.label: D
choices.text: identical to the premiere score.

choices.label: E
choices.text: replaced by spoken narration.

questions.explanation: The first paragraph says a single pianist might improvise from cue sheets.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward historical reconstruction can best be described as

choices.label: A
choices.text: appreciative but cautious.

choices.label: B
choices.text: openly hostile.

choices.label: C
choices.text: indifferent and dismissive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: enthusiastic without reservation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: uncertain whether it has ever occurred.

questions.explanation: The author calls it valuable but warns against a false sense of finality.

questions.stem: The phrase 'false sense of finality' refers to the mistaken idea that

choices.label: A
choices.text: a surviving premiere score must be the single definitive musical version of the film.

choices.label: B
choices.text: silent films always ended abruptly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: archives never preserve reliable documents.

choices.label: D
choices.text: modern audiences cannot understand live music.

choices.label: E
choices.text: a restored image is less important than a restored score.

questions.explanation: The next sentence explains that a premiere score was not necessarily definitive.

questions.stem: Which one of the following would the author most likely regard as a problem in a newly commissioned score?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It was written by a composer born long after the film's release.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It uses instruments unavailable at the film's premiere.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It overwhelms the film's pacing and visual movement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It lacks a surviving conductor's manuscript.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It was recorded rather than performed live.

questions.explanation: The author says the danger is music that draws attention away from the visual rhythm.

questions.stem: The author uses the example of different venues primarily to show that

choices.label: A
choices.text: silent-film music historically varied across performances.

choices.label: B
choices.text: small towns had better musicians than large cities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: silent films were not distributed outside major cities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: orchestras refused to play popular songs.

choices.label: E
choices.text: modern restorers should ignore archival evidence.

questions.explanation: The first paragraph emphasizes variation in live accompaniment.

questions.stem: Which one of the following is most analogous to the author's preferred approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A theater company stages a classical play using historical research while making performance choices that serve the play's dramatic structure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A museum locks a painting in storage to prevent anyone from interpreting it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A publisher replaces an old novel's ending because modern readers prefer happiness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A historian discards all primary sources because they are incomplete.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A lecturer reads a list of dates without discussing their significance.

questions.explanation: This mirrors the blend of historical evidence and responsible interpretation.

questions.stem: Both passages are primarily concerned with

choices.label: A
choices.text: whether vacant urban lots should be valued only according to their market price.

choices.label: B
choices.text: how cities should understand and use vacant lots that already have community functions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: whether gardens are more profitable than apartment buildings.

choices.label: D
choices.text: why tax bases are irrelevant to urban planning.

choices.label: E
choices.text: how residents can acquire private title to abandoned land.

questions.explanation: Both discuss vacant lots, existing community functions, and planning choices.

questions.stem: The author of Passage A would most likely criticize Passage B for

choices.label: A
choices.text: assuming that community stewardship can be adequately preserved through formal development requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: denying that cities need housing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: claiming that vacant lots have no social meaning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: rejecting all zoning agreements.

choices.label: E
choices.text: arguing that tax revenue is never relevant.

questions.explanation: Passage A worries official policy may miss local networks; it would likely doubt that formal agreements fully capture them.

questions.stem: The author of Passage B would most likely agree with which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Informal community use should always defeat proposals for housing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some community benefits of vacant lots can be incorporated into regulated development.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The only legitimate value of land is tax revenue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Housing shortages are created by block parties.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Public courtyards should be prohibited in new projects.

questions.explanation: Passage B says development can require courtyards and space for neighborhood groups.

questions.stem: Which one of the following best captures a central disagreement between the two passages?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether vacant lots can ever be used by residents.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether community use should weigh heavily enough to resist or reshape development.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether zoning agreements are legally possible.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether cities have governments.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether affordable housing can exist.

questions.explanation: Passage A prioritizes community stewardship; Passage B questions whether it justifies blocking broader development.

questions.stem: In Passage A, the phrase 'not appear on property ledgers' is used to emphasize that

choices.label: A
choices.text: some valuable uses of land are not captured by formal financial records.

choices.label: B
choices.text: residents are falsifying official documents.

choices.label: C
choices.text: property ledgers usually list block parties.

choices.label: D
choices.text: city officials are unable to read maps.

choices.label: E
choices.text: all vacant lots lack owners.

questions.explanation: The phrase contrasts social value with formal revenue or accounting measures.

questions.stem: Passage B's reference to people who need homes functions primarily to

choices.label: A
choices.text: introduce a broader public interest that may conflict with preserving informal local uses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prove that current neighborhood residents never need homes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: deny that vacant lots can host community activities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: criticize public courtyards.

choices.label: E
choices.text: argue that all lots should become offices.

questions.explanation: Passage B uses housing need to challenge preservation of informal arrangements.

questions.stem: Which one of the following proposals would most likely be favored by Passage B but viewed cautiously by Passage A?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Replacing a community-managed vacant lot with affordable housing that includes a courtyard and meeting room.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leaving every vacant lot unchanged forever regardless of housing demand.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Selling vacant lots for luxury storage units with no public access.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Banning residents from planting gardens anywhere in the city.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Removing all zoning rules for vacant land.

questions.explanation: Passage B supports inclusive regulated development; Passage A would worry about erasing informal networks.

sections.title: Logical Reasoning
sections.directions: Directions: Read each short passage and choose the answer that best responds to the question. Do not assume anything beyond what is stated unless the question asks for an assumption.

questions.stimulus: A city library extended its hours by two hours each evening. Visits increased, but borrowing did not. The library director concludes that the longer hours are not useful to patrons.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the director's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The library building is older than most buildings nearby.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Many evening visitors used study rooms, computers, or reference materials without borrowing books.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Borrowing increased at a different branch across town.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Some patrons dislike visiting libraries in the evening.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The library buys new books once each month.

questions.explanation: Usefulness need not be measured only by borrowing; patrons may benefit from services that do not involve checkout.

questions.stimulus: If the aquarium's new filtration system is operating properly, nitrate levels will remain below the safety threshold. Nitrate levels have exceeded the threshold. Therefore, the filtration system is not operating properly.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most closely parallels the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If the alarm is armed, the light flashes. The light is flashing. Therefore, the alarm is armed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If the store is open, the sign is lit. The sign is lit. Therefore, the store is open.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If the soup is boiling, steam rises. Steam is not rising. Therefore, the soup is not boiling.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If the road is icy, cars slow down. Cars are slowing down. Therefore, the road is icy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: If the play is sold out, no tickets remain. Some tickets remain. Therefore, the play is sold out.

questions.explanation: Both use modus tollens: if P then Q; not Q; therefore not P.

questions.stimulus: A study found that people who eat breakfast daily report less fatigue than people who skip breakfast. The author concludes that eating breakfast reduces fatigue.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the author's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some breakfast foods contain more protein than others.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The study included people of many ages.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fatigue is difficult to measure precisely.

choices.label: D
choices.text: People who sleep at least eight hours are more likely both to eat breakfast and to report less fatigue.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Most participants completed the survey on a weekday.

questions.explanation: Sleep could explain both breakfast eating and lower fatigue, undermining the claimed causal link.

questions.stimulus: The art school will offer printmaking next semester only if at least twelve students enroll. Exactly twelve students have enrolled. Therefore, the school will offer printmaking next semester.
questions.stem: The reasoning above is flawed because it

choices.label: A
choices.text: mistakes a sufficient condition for a necessary condition.

choices.label: B
choices.text: assumes that enrollment numbers never change.

choices.label: C
choices.text: relies on a survey of students not enrolled in art school.

choices.label: D
choices.text: draws a conclusion about all courses from evidence about one course.

choices.label: E
choices.text: treats a necessary condition as though it were sufficient.

questions.explanation: At least twelve students is required for offering, but the statement does not say it guarantees offering.

questions.stimulus: A town banned plastic bags, and litter cleanup costs fell by 10 percent the following year. The mayor says the ban saved taxpayers money. However, the town also hired a new cleanup contractor that charged lower rates.
questions.stem: Which one of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating the mayor's claim?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether the amount of litter collected changed after the bag ban.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether the mayor voted for the plastic bag ban.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether residents own reusable bags.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether cleanup workers wear uniforms.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether nearby towns also have plastic bag bans.

questions.explanation: If costs fell solely because of cheaper rates, the ban may not have reduced litter or saved money through cleanup reduction.

questions.stimulus: Paleontologist: The fossil cannot belong to Species K because it has a ridge on the jaw, and no known Species K fossil has such a ridge.
questions.stem: The paleontologist's argument assumes which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every fossil with a jaw ridge belongs to the same species.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Known Species K fossils are sufficiently representative to show that Species K lacked jaw ridges.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Species K lived in only one geographic region.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The fossil is complete except for the jaw.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fossils with jaw ridges are never found near Species K fossils.

questions.explanation: The inference from no known examples to no true examples requires representativeness of the known fossil record.

questions.stimulus: A novelist says that writers should not revise drafts because the first version best preserves the original inspiration. Yet the novelist's most praised book was revised seven times before publication.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes the force of the second sentence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It proves that all novels should be revised seven times.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It shows that inspiration never matters to writing quality.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It offers evidence that the novelist has not consistently followed the stated advice.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It explains why the novelist dislikes revision.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It identifies the main conclusion of the argument.

questions.explanation: The second sentence points out tension between the novelist's stated view and the novelist's own successful practice.

questions.stimulus: A coffee shop began allowing customers to order through an app. The average wait time fell from seven minutes to five minutes. Therefore, every customer is now served faster than before.
questions.stem: Which one of the following identifies the flaw in the reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It treats app orders as less important than in-person orders.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It assumes that all customers have smartphones.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It concludes that wait time matters more than coffee quality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It infers a conclusion about every customer from a decrease in the average wait time.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It overlooks the fact that five minutes is still a wait.

questions.explanation: A lower average does not guarantee that each individual wait time decreased.

questions.stimulus: Some species of moss survive freezing by producing a protective sugar. The Arctic moss sample survived repeated freezing in the laboratory. Therefore, the sample must produce that protective sugar.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Arctic moss sample was collected during the summer.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The laboratory freezer was calibrated before the experiment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Protective sugars are found in some flowering plants.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sample was green when it was collected.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some mosses survive freezing through a different mechanism involving cell-wall flexibility.

questions.explanation: An alternative freezing-survival mechanism undermines the claim that the sugar must be present.

questions.stimulus: A company will approve remote work for an employee if the employee's job can be performed securely off-site and the employee has no recent performance warnings. Luis has no recent performance warnings, but his job cannot be performed securely off-site.
questions.stem: Which one of the following can be properly inferred?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Luis will not be approved for remote work under the stated policy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Luis will be approved for remote work.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any employee without performance warnings will be approved for remote work.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No employee with performance warnings can work securely off-site.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Luis's job is less important than jobs that can be performed off-site.

questions.explanation: Both conditions are required. Luis lacks one of them, so he does not qualify under the policy.

questions.stimulus: An economist argues that raising the minimum wage in the province did not reduce employment because the total number of jobs rose afterward. But the province's population also grew substantially during that period.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the economist's argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Several neighboring provinces have lower minimum wages.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The employment rate, not merely the number of jobs, rose after the wage increase.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Many workers earn more than the minimum wage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The wage increase was debated for several months before being adopted.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some employers opposed the wage increase.

questions.explanation: An increased employment rate accounts for population growth better than raw job totals do.

questions.stimulus: Professor: Students learn legal writing best by receiving detailed feedback on drafts. The new seminar provides detailed feedback on three drafts. Therefore, students in the new seminar will learn legal writing better than students in any course that provides no draft feedback.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is a sufficient assumption for the professor's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Students prefer seminars to lecture courses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The new seminar enrolls fewer students than most courses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No course that provides no draft feedback uses any method that teaches legal writing as well as detailed feedback on drafts does.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Detailed feedback takes instructors a long time to provide.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Students in the new seminar already know basic grammar.

questions.explanation: This closes the gap by ruling out equally effective alternative methods in courses without draft feedback.

questions.stimulus: A historian claims that a recently discovered diary was written by Governor Han because it describes a private meeting that only Han attended. But the diary also describes events that occurred two years after Han's death.
questions.stem: The information about events after Han's death most strongly supports which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The private meeting never occurred.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Governor Han did not keep any diaries.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The diary's handwriting is impossible to analyze.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The diary was not written entirely by Governor Han.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The diary was written by one of Han's political rivals.

questions.explanation: Han could not have written entries about events that occurred after his death, so the diary was not entirely his work.

questions.stimulus: The theater sold out Friday's performance after a popular actor was added to the cast. The manager concludes that adding the actor caused the sellout. However, the performance was also the final show of the season.
questions.stem: Which one of the following would most help evaluate the manager's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether the actor has performed in theaters before.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether Friday's performance began on time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the theater sells snacks during intermission.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether the actor preferred the role.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether final shows in previous seasons sold out without the popular actor.

questions.explanation: Past final-show sellouts would suggest an alternative explanation for the sellout.

questions.stimulus: All manuscripts in the archive that are written on blue paper are cataloged in Collection R. Some manuscripts in Collection R are unsigned. Therefore, some manuscripts written on blue paper are unsigned.
questions.stem: The conclusion does not follow because the premises leave open the possibility that

choices.label: A
choices.text: all blue-paper manuscripts are signed, while only non-blue-paper manuscripts in Collection R are unsigned.

choices.label: B
choices.text: no manuscripts are cataloged in Collection R.

choices.label: C
choices.text: some unsigned manuscripts are written on blue paper.

choices.label: D
choices.text: all manuscripts in the archive are written on blue paper.

choices.label: E
choices.text: blue paper was used only in one century.

questions.explanation: Collection R may contain both blue-paper and non-blue-paper manuscripts; the unsigned ones need not be blue-paper manuscripts.

questions.stimulus: A town council member argues: The new fountain should be built because it will beautify the square. It will beautify the square because it is beautiful.
questions.stem: The reasoning in the council member's argument is flawed because it

choices.label: A
choices.text: draws a general conclusion from a single example.

choices.label: B
choices.text: takes for granted the very point it is trying to establish.

choices.label: C
choices.text: confuses a cause with an effect.

choices.label: D
choices.text: attacks an opponent rather than an opponent's argument.

choices.label: E
choices.text: assumes that what is popular is morally right.

questions.explanation: The argument says the fountain will beautify the square because it is beautiful, which simply restates the conclusion in different words.

questions.stimulus: A medical clinic found that patients who received appointment reminders missed fewer appointments. The clinic concluded that reminders reduce missed appointments. The reminders were sent only to patients who requested them.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the clinic's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The reminders were sent by text message rather than email.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some patients scheduled appointments in the morning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patients who requested reminders were already more careful about keeping appointments than other patients were.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The clinic has more doctors now than it did last year.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The clinic charges a fee for missed appointments.

questions.explanation: Self-selection could explain the lower missed-appointment rate without reminders being the cause.

questions.stimulus: Policy analyst: If a regulation's compliance costs exceed its public benefits, the regulation should be revised. The harbor rule's compliance costs exceed its public benefits. Therefore, the harbor rule should be revised.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most closely matches the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If a song is old, it may be familiar. This song is familiar. Therefore, it is old.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If a file is encrypted, it is protected. This file is protected. Therefore, it is encrypted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If a course is full, registration is difficult. This course is not full. Therefore, registration is not difficult.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If a bridge is unsafe, it should be closed. The Old Mill Bridge is unsafe. Therefore, it should be closed.

choices.label: E
choices.text: If a painting is framed, it can be hung. This painting can be hung. Therefore, it is framed.

questions.explanation: Both arguments apply a general conditional rule to a specific case that satisfies the condition.

questions.stimulus: A newspaper editorial says that because the city's oldest bridge has lasted for 110 years, it will last for another 110 years without major repairs.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the strongest criticism of the editorial's reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It concludes that all bridges in the city are equally old.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It ignores whether citizens enjoy the bridge's design.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It relies on expert testimony that the bridge is unsafe.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It assumes repairs are always inexpensive.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It assumes that past durability guarantees equal future durability despite aging and changed conditions.

questions.explanation: Longevity so far does not by itself show that the bridge can continue safely without repairs.

questions.stimulus: The county's recycling rate increased after bins were placed in parks. However, household recycling decreased during the same period.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, best resolves the apparent discrepancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The increase in park recycling was larger than the decrease in household recycling.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Park visitors often bring reusable water bottles.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The county changed the color of recycling bins.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Household recycling is collected on Tuesdays.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some residents do not visit county parks.

questions.explanation: The overall rate can rise if park recycling increases by more than household recycling falls.

questions.stimulus: A laptop review says Model Z is best for students because it has the longest battery life of any laptop tested. The review does not discuss weight, price, durability, or software compatibility.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a weakness in the review's reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It denies that battery life matters to students.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It assumes one desirable feature is enough to establish overall suitability for students.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It compares Model Z with laptops rather than tablets.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It relies on student surveys rather than lab tests.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It claims that all students use the same software.

questions.explanation: The conclusion about being best for students is broader than the evidence about battery life alone.

questions.stimulus: Every novel on the shortlist was published this year. No book published this year is eligible for the lifetime achievement award. Therefore, no novel on the shortlist is eligible for the lifetime achievement award.
questions.stem: The reasoning in the argument is

choices.label: A
choices.text: flawed, because it assumes all books are novels.

choices.label: B
choices.text: flawed, because it treats an exception as a rule.

choices.label: C
choices.text: valid, because the premises guarantee the conclusion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: flawed, because it overlooks unpublished novels.

choices.label: E
choices.text: valid only if the shortlist contains exactly five novels.

questions.explanation: If every shortlisted novel was published this year and no book published this year is eligible, then no shortlisted novel is eligible.

questions.stimulus: A software firm says it improved customer support because average response time fell. Yet the number of unresolved support tickets at the end of each week increased.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, best explains how both statements could be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The firm hired several support agents during the year.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some customers submit tickets outside business hours.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The firm sells software to businesses and individuals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The firm responded quickly with automated acknowledgments but took longer to solve complex problems.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The firm's support agents work remotely.

questions.explanation: Fast initial responses can reduce response time while unresolved tickets pile up if resolution is slow.

questions.stimulus: A chef argues that because locally grown tomatoes taste better than shipped tomatoes, the restaurant should buy only local tomatoes. But local tomatoes are available for only three months each year.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most supports the chef's recommendation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tomatoes are botanically fruits rather than vegetables.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some customers dislike tomato dishes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Shipped tomatoes are sold by many suppliers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The restaurant uses herbs grown in another state.

choices.label: E
choices.text: During the months local tomatoes are unavailable, the restaurant can adjust its menu to avoid dishes requiring fresh tomatoes.

questions.explanation: The availability objection is answered if the restaurant can avoid needing fresh tomatoes outside the local season.

questions.stimulus: A climate model predicted that a region would experience more intense rainstorms, and last year the region had its most intense rainstorm on record. Therefore, the model is accurate.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the strongest criticism of the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It judges the accuracy of a broad predictive model from a single event.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It assumes that rainstorms cannot be measured.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It rejects models that make probabilistic predictions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It concludes that the region is colder than before.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It ignores the fact that records are written down.

questions.explanation: One matching event is insufficient to establish that the overall model is accurate.

sections.title: Logical Reasoning
sections.status: Variable / Unscored for this practice test
sections.directions: Directions: Read each short passage and choose the answer that best responds to the question. Do not assume anything beyond what is stated unless the question asks for an assumption.

questions.stimulus: A bakery introduced a loyalty card, and repeat visits increased. The owner concludes the card caused the increase. At the same time, a competing bakery nearby closed.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most weakens the owner's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some customers lost their loyalty cards.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The bakery sells more than one kind of bread.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The loyalty card is made of paper.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The competing bakery's closure could have sent more customers to the bakery regardless of the card.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The owner has worked at the bakery for ten years.

questions.explanation: The competitor's closure is an alternative explanation for the rise in repeat visits.

questions.stimulus: If the town approves the zoning change, then the warehouse project can proceed. The warehouse project cannot proceed. Therefore, the town did not approve the zoning change.
questions.stem: The reasoning above is

choices.label: A
choices.text: flawed, because it affirms the necessary condition.

choices.label: B
choices.text: flawed, because it assumes the zoning change is unpopular.

choices.label: C
choices.text: valid only if warehouses are always profitable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: flawed, because it ignores environmental concerns.

choices.label: E
choices.text: valid, because it denies the necessary result of the approval.

questions.explanation: The argument has the form if P then Q; not Q; therefore not P.

questions.stimulus: A dermatologist says a sunscreen is effective because it blocks 98 percent of UVB rays. However, the sunscreen washes off easily in water, and most buyers use it while swimming.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the strongest criticism of the dermatologist's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It ignores whether the sunscreen remains effective under the conditions in which it is commonly used.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It assumes swimmers never use towels.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It claims UVB rays are harmless.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It compares sunscreen with hats.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It relies on a sample that is too large.

questions.explanation: Effectiveness in general is undermined if the product fails under typical use conditions.

questions.stimulus: A magazine's article on home gardening was shared more widely than any article the magazine has published. The editor concludes that home gardening is the topic readers care about most.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the editor's conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Many readers have balconies rather than yards.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The article was shared widely because it contained an amusing photograph unrelated to gardening.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The magazine has published articles for twenty years.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The editor enjoys gardening at home.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some readers printed the article.

questions.explanation: Shares may reflect interest in the photograph rather than interest in the article's topic.

questions.stimulus: Every sculpture in the east wing is made of bronze. Some bronze artworks in the museum were acquired before 1950. Therefore, some sculptures in the east wing were acquired before 1950.
questions.stem: The conclusion does not follow because

choices.label: A
choices.text: some sculptures may be made of marble.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the museum may own more than one east wing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the bronze artworks acquired before 1950 need not include any sculptures in the east wing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: bronze can be polished.

choices.label: E
choices.text: all artworks acquired before 1950 are sculptures.

questions.explanation: The overlap between east-wing sculptures and pre-1950 bronze artworks is not established.

questions.stimulus: A principal says the new attendance policy improved learning because absences declined after it was adopted. Test scores, however, also rose in schools that did not adopt the policy.
questions.stem: Which one of the following would most help evaluate the principal's reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether the policy was printed in the student handbook.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether students like the principal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether test scores are reported online.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether absences declined more at the principal's school than at otherwise similar schools without the policy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether teachers park near the school.

questions.explanation: The comparison helps determine whether the policy made a difference beyond broader trends.

questions.stimulus: The city should plant shade trees on bus routes because shade lowers summer temperatures at bus stops. Cooler bus stops will make transit more comfortable.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some trees lose leaves during winter.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Many bus routes have printed schedules.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The city has planted flowers downtown.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bus shelters are usually made of metal and glass.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Most riders wait outdoors long enough for temperature at the stop to affect their comfort.

questions.explanation: The argument requires that stop temperature matter to riders' comfort during actual waits.
