questions.stimulus: A researcher found that bilingual children in one school solved puzzles faster than monolingual children. She concludes that bilingualism improves puzzle-solving ability.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The bilingual children had all participated in an after-school puzzle club.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some monolingual children enjoy puzzles.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The puzzles used words and pictures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The school offers language classes.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The researcher timed the children with a stopwatch.

questions.explanation: Puzzle-club participation provides an alternative explanation for faster puzzle solving.

questions.stimulus: A law states that any restaurant serving raw oysters must post a warning. Cafe Nola serves raw oysters. Therefore, Cafe Nola must post a warning.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most closely parallels the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Any athlete who trains improves. Dana improved. Therefore, Dana trained.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Any vehicle parked overnight needs a permit. This van is parked overnight. Therefore, this van needs a permit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any glass dropped may break. This glass broke. Therefore, it was dropped.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Any letter mailed today arrives tomorrow. This letter arrived tomorrow. Therefore, it was mailed today.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Any plant watered grows. This plant is not watered. Therefore, it will not grow.

questions.explanation: Both arguments apply a universal rule to an item that satisfies the condition.

questions.stimulus: A movie critic argues that a sequel is unnecessary because the original film told a complete story. But a sequel can tell a new story using the same characters without changing whether the original story was complete.
questions.stem: The critic's reasoning is most vulnerable because it assumes that

choices.label: A
choices.text: viewers always prefer sequels to original films.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the original film had no characters.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a sequel is justified only if the original story was incomplete.

choices.label: D
choices.text: complete stories are never enjoyable.

choices.label: E
choices.text: critics should write reviews before seeing films.

questions.explanation: The objection only works if incompleteness is required to justify a sequel.

questions.stimulus: No report written by the finance committee is confidential. Some confidential reports were released yesterday. Therefore, some reports released yesterday were not written by the finance committee.
questions.stem: Which one of the following describes the reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is flawed: it assumes all released reports are confidential.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is flawed: it denies that reports can be released.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is valid only if the finance committee released no reports yesterday.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is valid: the confidential reports released yesterday cannot be finance-committee reports.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is flawed: it confuses reports with committees.

questions.explanation: If no finance-committee reports are confidential, then any confidential report was not written by that committee.

questions.stimulus: A botanist argues that a plant found on the island is native because it grows in several remote valleys. However, birds can carry the plant's seeds long distances.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes the weakness identified by the second sentence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The plant cannot grow in valleys.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All island plants are carried by birds.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Native plants never spread by seed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The botanist has studied birds rather than plants.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The plant's remote locations may not show native origin if seeds could have been transported there naturally.

questions.explanation: Seed dispersal by birds undermines the inference from remoteness to native status.

questions.stimulus: A city claims its public art program increased tourism because hotel bookings rose after the murals were installed. But a national sports tournament was held in the city during the same month.
questions.stem: Which one of the following would most help evaluate the city's claim?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether hotel bookings remained higher in months after the tournament ended.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether artists used bright colors in the murals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether tourists usually take photographs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether some hotels are near the train station.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether the tournament had a souvenir shop.

questions.explanation: If the effect persists after the tournament, the murals become a more plausible cause.

questions.stimulus: If the laboratory sample was contaminated, then the test result is unreliable. The test result is reliable. Therefore, the sample was not contaminated.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the form of the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Affirming the consequent.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Denying the consequent.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Circular reasoning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: False analogy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ad hominem reasoning.

questions.explanation: The argument uses if P then Q; not Q; therefore not P.

questions.stimulus: A delivery company says its new routing software reduced fuel use because fuel purchases fell after installation. Yet the company also sold 20 percent of its trucks.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The trucks that were sold were painted blue.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The software has a simple interface.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fuel use per mile driven fell after the software was installed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Some drivers prefer paper maps.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The company delivers packages in several cities.

questions.explanation: Fuel use per mile accounts for changes in fleet size better than total purchases do.

questions.stimulus: A student concludes that Professor Singh grades easily because everyone in her seminar received an A. But enrollment in the seminar required applicants to submit excellent prior research papers.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes the flaw?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It assumes grades can be expressed only as letters.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It criticizes a professor's character rather than grading standards.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It concludes that all professors grade easily.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It ignores the possibility that the students were unusually strong before taking the seminar.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It overlooks the number of books assigned in the seminar.

questions.explanation: Selective enrollment could explain the high grades without easy grading.

questions.stimulus: A nutritionist argues that because lentils are inexpensive and rich in protein, the university cafeteria should offer lentil dishes daily. The cafeteria's current menu lacks affordable protein options for vegetarian students.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lentils have been cultivated for centuries.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some students are not vegetarian.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The cafeteria sells coffee every morning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Protein can also be found in dairy products.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Many vegetarian students would choose lentil dishes if they were available.

questions.explanation: The recommendation is stronger if the target group would actually use the proposed option.

questions.stimulus: The new law applies to vehicles that weigh more than 10,000 pounds. The delivery van weighs 9,500 pounds. Therefore, the new law does not apply to the delivery van.
questions.stem: The argument's conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The law applies only to vehicles that weigh more than 10,000 pounds.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The delivery van is used for business purposes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Most vehicles weigh less than 10,000 pounds.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The new law concerns driver training.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The van's weight changes with cargo.

questions.explanation: The premise gives a sufficient condition for application; the conclusion requires that it also be necessary.

questions.stimulus: A survey found that residents who attend neighborhood meetings feel more informed about city issues. The surveyor concludes that meetings make residents informed.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some meetings include refreshments.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Residents already interested in city issues are more likely to attend meetings.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The survey was conducted online.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The city holds meetings in the evening.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Residents disagree about parking rules.

questions.explanation: Preexisting interest could cause both attendance and feeling informed.

questions.stimulus: A restaurant's chef claims the new menu is more popular because total orders increased. But the restaurant extended its hours and served more customers overall.
questions.stem: Which one of the following would most help evaluate the chef's claim?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether the chef trained at a culinary school.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether the restaurant changed its tablecloths.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the percentage of customers ordering items from the new menu increased.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether some customers order water.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether dinner is busier than lunch.

questions.explanation: Total orders may rise simply because more customers are served; percentage choice better measures menu popularity.

questions.stimulus: A columnist says electric buses cannot reduce city emissions because producing their batteries creates emissions. The columnist ignores emissions from operating diesel buses over many years.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes the problem with the columnist's reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It assumes batteries can be produced without minerals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It claims diesel buses have no passengers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It concludes that all batteries are identical.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It considers one source of emissions while neglecting another relevant source for comparison.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It relies on a definition of city that is too broad.

questions.explanation: A fair comparison must consider total lifecycle or relevant operational emissions, not only battery production.

questions.stimulus: The orchestra will perform the concerto only if a guest pianist is available. A guest pianist is available. Therefore, the orchestra will perform the concerto.
questions.stem: The reasoning is flawed because it

choices.label: A
choices.text: uses a conclusion as evidence for itself.

choices.label: B
choices.text: rejects a claim because of its source.

choices.label: C
choices.text: draws a comparison between unlike cases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: infers a cause from a correlation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: confuses a necessary condition with a sufficient condition.

questions.explanation: Availability is necessary for the concerto, but the statement does not say availability guarantees performance.

questions.stimulus: An engineer recommends replacing a bridge's sensors because they are ten years old. The sensors are designed to operate for twenty years, and recent tests show they still measure accurately.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the engineer's recommendation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sensors have not reached their designed service life and are currently accurate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The bridge crosses a river.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some sensors are more expensive than others.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The engineer has worked on bridges before.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The sensors were installed in the spring.

questions.explanation: The stated reason for replacement is age, but the sensors are within their design life and functioning accurately.

questions.stimulus: A bookstore owner noticed that customers who receive staff recommendations buy more books. She concludes that recommendations cause customers to buy more. Staff, however, usually recommend books only to customers who ask many questions about books.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some staff recommendations are for paperbacks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Customers who ask many questions are already more likely to buy books.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The bookstore has a children's section.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The owner tracks purchases with receipts.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Many books have colorful covers.

questions.explanation: The staff recommendations may be correlated with preexisting customer interest rather than causing purchases.

questions.stimulus: A historian argues that a treaty was unpopular because many newspapers criticized it. Yet most newspapers at the time were owned by a small group of merchants who opposed the treaty for financial reasons.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the strongest criticism of the historian's argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It assumes treaties cannot affect merchants.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It denies that newspapers existed at the time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It treats newspaper criticism as representative public opinion despite possible ownership bias.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It relies on private letters rather than printed sources.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It concludes that merchants never agree with the public.

questions.explanation: The newspaper sample may reflect concentrated ownership interests rather than broad public sentiment.

sections.title: Logical Reasoning
sections.directions: Directions: Read each short passage and choose the answer that best responds to the question. Do not assume anything beyond what is stated unless the question asks you to do so.

questions.stimulus: In a winter budget review of a nonprofit program, after Harborview added electric ferries during morning commute, weekday bridge congestion fell by 12 percent. City officials therefore concluded that the change caused the improvement and that the same measure should be expanded to all comparable programs next year.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It assumes that a policy must be perfect in order to be worth considering.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It treats a before-and-after change as proof of causation without ruling out other causes or showing that the programs are comparable.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It rejects numerical evidence merely because the evidence is statistical.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It confuses the popularity of a proposal with its legality.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It concludes that a result is impossible because it has not yet occurred.

questions.explanation: The argument moves from a temporal association to causation and then to a broader recommendation without addressing alternatives or comparability.

questions.stimulus: In a annual operations review of a institutional program, a policy analyst argues that kept two branches open until 10 p.m. caused the reported improvement because, in the first full year after the change, adult card renewals rose sharply. The analyst notes that the measurement method was the same in both years.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some residents said they had not heard about the policy until months after it began.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The policy was less expensive to administer than officials had predicted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Several neighboring jurisdictions considered a similar policy but did not adopt it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No other major change affecting the measured result occurred in Merriton during the comparison period.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The analyst personally favored the policy before reviewing the data.

questions.explanation: Ruling out other major changes makes the policy a more plausible explanation for the improvement.

questions.stimulus: In a pilot-phase procurement review of a campus program, the director of the school lunch redesign says the initiative was successful: after Pine County moved fruit displays to the front of cafeterias, fruit purchases increased. The director concludes that the initiative itself produced the improvement.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: During the same period, the district also lowered fruit prices, which would independently tend to produce the same reported result.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The initiative cost less than the director initially expected.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some residents were unaware that the initiative had begun.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The director had supported similar initiatives in other places.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The reported result was measured in percentages rather than raw numbers.

questions.explanation: An independent cause of the same result weakens the claim that the initiative itself produced the improvement.

questions.stimulus: In a regional accessibility review of a neighborhood program, the committee should treat the safety waiver as valid for two years, since it is marked confidential. Every document that is marked confidential is normally processed quickly.
questions.stem: The argument depends on assuming which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No document that is not marked confidential is ever processed quickly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The committee has reviewed more safety waivers than any other kind of document.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Being processed quickly is enough, in this case, for the safety waiver to be treated as valid for two years.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Documents treated as valid for two years are always controversial.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The person who submitted the safety waiver expected it to be processed quickly.

questions.explanation: The premise establishes quick processing; the conclusion needs a bridge from quick processing to the special treatment recommended.

questions.stimulus: In a summer implementation review of a municipal program, any membership record that is stored in the main archive is listed on the public website. Any membership record that is listed on the public website must be logged by the records office. Eleni's membership record was not logged by the records office.
questions.stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Eleni's membership record was listed on the public website.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No membership record is logged unless it is stored in the main archive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every logged membership record is stored in the main archive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Eleni's membership record was the only one not logged by the records office.

choices.label: E

sections.title: Reading Comprehension
sections.directions: Directions: Each set of questions is based on a single passage or a pair of related passages. Answer each question on the basis of what is stated or implied in the passage.

passages.title: Community Courts and Algorithmic Triage
passages.text:
Community courts were originally promoted as a way to respond to low-level offenses with services rather than with ordinary punishment. A person charged with sleeping in a subway station, for example, might be directed to housing assistance instead of simply receiving a fine. Recently, some cities have proposed using algorithmic triage tools to sort cases quickly, assigning defendants to service tracks based on prior contacts with courts, housing records, and other administrative data.

Supporters argue that triage tools can reduce arbitrariness. When court staff are overloaded, similar cases may receive different treatment depending on which clerk happens to be available. A shared scoring system, supporters say, makes the initial sorting more consistent and can reserve scarce social-service appointments for people most likely to need them.

The concern is not merely that algorithms can be inaccurate, although they can. The deeper problem is that the data used by such tools often record previous government attention rather than underlying need. Someone who has frequently been stopped by police may look riskier than someone whose similar conduct has gone unnoticed. If a community court imports those patterns without scrutiny, it may convert unequal observation into an apparently neutral score.

A defensible system would therefore make human choices visible. Designers should identify which records are used, explain why each record is relevant to the court's goals, and allow judges to depart from scores when local knowledge shows that the score is misleading. Used this way, a triage tool might assist a court. Treated as an objective substitute for judgment, it would undermine the very responsiveness community courts were meant to provide.

passages.title: Desert Varnish
passages.text:
Across many arid landscapes, exposed stones acquire a thin dark coating known as desert varnish. For decades, geologists described the coating mainly as a slow chemical deposit of manganese and iron drawn from dust and dew. That account explained why varnish often appears on stable rock surfaces and why it can take thousands of years to develop.

More recent studies have complicated this picture. Microscopic channels and organic residues within some varnish layers suggest that bacteria or fungi may participate in concentrating metals. But the evidence is uneven. Some varnish contains biological traces; some does not. Moreover, a biological trace in a layer does not prove that organisms built the layer. Microbes may colonize an already forming surface rather than initiate it.

The most persuasive current view is therefore pluralistic. Chemical processes may supply and oxidize metals, while organisms may accelerate deposition under some conditions. The balance likely varies with humidity, dust composition, rock type, and the stability of the exposed surface. Desert varnish is less a single recipe than a family of slow surface histories.

This pluralism matters because researchers sometimes use varnish to estimate how long a rock surface has been exposed. If varnish formation varies by setting, then a dark coating cannot simply be read as a universal clock. It can still be evidence, but only when calibrated with local geology and with an appreciation for the different pathways by which the coating may have formed.

passages.title: Translating Oral Epics
passages.text:
A printed translation of an oral epic can create an illusion of fixity. The reader sees a bound book with numbered lines and may assume that the translator has captured a stable original. But many oral epics are not fixed texts. Singers adapt episodes to audience, occasion, and memory, drawing from inherited formulas while improvising transitions and emphases.

Older translators often tried to make such performances resemble literary epics familiar to European readers. Repetition was trimmed, abrupt turns were smoothed, and local references were replaced with explanatory generalities. These choices made the poems more accessible, but they also muted the artistry of performance. Repetition, for instance, can mark a singer's control of tempo or invite audience participation; it is not merely redundancy.

Recent translators have experimented with formats that acknowledge performance. Some preserve recurring formulas even at the risk of seeming strange on the page. Others include notes on gesture, audience response, or alternate versions of an episode. These strategies cannot reproduce the original event, but they can prevent the book from pretending that no event occurred.

The translator's task, then, is not to choose between literal accuracy and readability as though those were the only values. A translation should also disclose its own conditions: who performed the poem, when, before what audience, and with what known variations. Such disclosure does not solve every problem, but it reminds readers that the epic lives partly in repeated performance, not merely in preserved wording.

passages.title: Comparative Reading - University Endowments and Divestment
passages.text:
Passage A: University endowments are not ordinary investment funds. They exist to support teaching and research across generations, but they also express institutional priorities. When a university invests in companies whose activities sharply conflict with its publicly stated mission, it teaches one lesson in classrooms and another through its portfolio. Divestment is not a magic lever that can by itself transform an industry. Its value is partly declarative: it clarifies that some profits are incompatible with the institution's identity. Critics who demand immediate measurable market effects misunderstand the role of institutional judgment.

Passage B: Endowment managers should be cautious about divestment campaigns. A university may have many moral commitments, but the endowment's first responsibility is to provide stable support for scholarships, libraries, laboratories, and salaries. Once investment decisions become referenda on every contested issue, managers risk sacrificing diversification and inviting pressure from whichever group is currently loudest. Universities can express values through research, teaching, and direct spending without turning the endowment into a symbolic notice board. Divestment may sometimes be justified, but only when the financial risks and governance standards are carefully specified.

choices.text: dust and dew are present in arid environments.

choices.label: E
choices.text: some varnish layers are very thin.

questions.explanation: The second paragraph warns that microbes may arrive after a surface has begun forming.

questions.stem: The author uses the phrase “family of slow surface histories” to emphasize that desert varnish

choices.label: A
choices.text: may arise through several related but locally variable processes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: is created only by inherited genetic traits.

choices.label: C
choices.text: forms quickly during rare rainstorms.

choices.label: D
choices.text: contains no chemical components.

choices.label: E
choices.text: is identical on all stones in a desert.

questions.explanation: The phrase captures the pluralistic view of varied pathways.

questions.stem: Which one of the following would most weaken the pluralistic view described in the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Researchers find that varnish formation rates vary with humidity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A survey shows that all sampled varnish layers worldwide have the same structure and form at the same rate by the same nonbiological mechanism.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some varnish layers contain organic residues.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dust in different deserts contains different amounts of manganese.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A dark coating on one rock surface is older than a lighter coating nearby.

questions.explanation: Uniform global structure and mechanism would undermine local variation and pluralism.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that using varnish as a “universal clock” would be problematic because

choices.label: A
choices.text: formation rates and mechanisms may vary by local conditions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: varnish is never older than the rock beneath it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: geologists cannot identify manganese in the laboratory.

choices.label: D
choices.text: all rocks in deserts are constantly moved by rivers.

choices.label: E
choices.text: dark surfaces are always recently exposed.

questions.explanation: The final paragraph says the coating must be calibrated locally.

questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes the author's attitude toward desert varnish as evidence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cautiously accepting: useful when interpreted in context.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dismissive: worthless for any scientific purpose.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enthusiastic: a perfect dating method in all deserts.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Indifferent: irrelevant to geology.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hostile: a topic that should not be studied.

questions.explanation: The author says it can still be evidence, but only with calibration and caution.

questions.stem: The main point of the passage is that translators of oral epics should

choices.label: A
choices.text: try to represent the performative and variable nature of the poems, not merely produce a smooth fixed text.

choices.label: B
choices.text: remove repetition whenever it would slow down modern readers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: translate only those oral epics that already have a single written original.

choices.label: D
choices.text: avoid notes because they interrupt literary enjoyment.

choices.label: E
choices.text: replace local references with generalities to broaden appeal.

questions.explanation: The author argues for acknowledging performance, variation, and conditions of translation.

questions.stem: The author mentions older translators primarily to

choices.label: A
choices.text: contrast an approach that increased readability with one that could obscure performance-based artistry.

choices.label: B
choices.text: show that European readers disliked literary epics.

choices.label: C
choices.text: prove that all early translations were intentionally deceptive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: argue that repetition is always impossible to translate.

choices.label: E
choices.text: explain why oral epics stopped being performed.

questions.explanation: The second paragraph contrasts smoothing strategies with their artistic cost.

questions.stem: According to the passage, repetition in an oral epic may

choices.label: A
choices.text: serve performance functions such as controlling tempo or inviting audience participation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prove that the singer forgot the story.

choices.label: C
choices.text: show that the poem has no inherited formulas.

choices.label: D
choices.text: make translation unnecessary.

choices.label: E
choices.text: indicate that the printed version is the only authoritative version.

questions.explanation: The passage explicitly gives tempo and participation as possible functions.

questions.stem: Which one of the following would best exemplify the translation strategy favored by the author?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A translation that preserves recurring phrases and includes a note explaining when the singer varied them in different performances.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A translation that silently removes local place names to make the poem more universal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A translation that rewrites the poem as a modern novel without identifying the performer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A translation that deletes all audience responses as irrelevant.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A translation that presents one performance as the timeless original.

questions.explanation: This preserves formulaic recurrence and discloses performance variation.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that “literal accuracy and readability” are

choices.label: A
choices.text: important but insufficient values for translating oral epic.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the only values any responsible translator should consider.

choices.label: C
choices.text: mutually impossible in every translation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: irrelevant because oral epics cannot be translated.

choices.label: E
choices.text: less important than making the poem resemble European literature.

questions.explanation: The final paragraph says the task is not just choosing between those two values.

questions.stem: The author would most likely view a translation that omits all information about performer, occasion, and audience as

choices.label: A
choices.text: incomplete because it hides conditions central to understanding the epic as performance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: superior because it lets the poem stand entirely alone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: necessary whenever the poem contains repetition.

choices.label: D
choices.text: acceptable only if the translation is printed in numbered lines.

choices.label: E
choices.text: proof that oral poetry is actually written literature.

questions.explanation: The author wants translations to disclose those conditions.

questions.stem: The function of the third paragraph is to

choices.label: A
choices.text: describe newer translation practices that partly address problems identified in the previous paragraph.

choices.label: B
choices.text: argue that no printed translation can ever be useful.

choices.label: C
choices.text: summarize the plot of a famous oral epic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: show that audience response is more important than wording in every case.

choices.label: E
choices.text: reject all attempts to preserve recurring formulas.

questions.explanation: The third paragraph presents recent strategies responding to earlier smoothing.

questions.stem: Both passages are concerned with

choices.label: A
choices.text: whether university endowments should be used to express institutional values through investment choices.

choices.label: B
choices.text: whether universities should stop funding libraries.

choices.label: C
choices.text: how individual students should choose majors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: whether all companies in an industry have the same business model.

choices.label: E
choices.text: how to calculate the market price of a stock.

questions.explanation: Both address the relationship between endowment investment and institutional values.

questions.stem: The author of Passage A would most likely respond to the “symbolic notice board” criticism in Passage B by saying that

choices.label: A
choices.text: symbolic expression can be a legitimate part of institutional judgment, especially when investments conflict with mission.

choices.label: B
choices.text: endowments should ignore every financial consideration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: universities have no public missions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: divestment always produces immediate industry-wide change.

choices.label: E
choices.text: endowment managers should follow the loudest campaign automatically.

questions.explanation: Passage A defends declarative value and rejects the demand for immediate market effects.

questions.stem: Passage B differs from Passage A in emphasizing

choices.label: A
choices.text: the endowment's duty to provide stable financial support and the governance risks of politicized investment decisions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the impossibility of measuring scholarship spending.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the claim that divestment is never requested by students.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the view that universities have no moral commitments.

choices.label: E
choices.text: the need to invest only in local companies.

questions.explanation: Passage B focuses on financial support, diversification, and governance standards.

questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes the relationship between the passages?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Passage A defends divestment as institutional expression; Passage B warns that such expression can threaten financial and governance responsibilities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Passage A and Passage B both reject divestment under all circumstances.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Passage A explains a scientific theory that Passage B applies to law.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Passage B offers historical evidence proving Passage A factually wrong.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Passage A argues for secrecy while Passage B argues for disclosure.

questions.explanation: The two passages take contrasting but not absolute positions.

questions.stem: The author of Passage B would most likely agree that divestment may be acceptable when

choices.label: A
choices.text: clear standards and financial risks have been carefully considered.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a campaign becomes loud enough to embarrass administrators.

choices.label: C
choices.text: it produces no administrative work.

choices.label: D
choices.text: it allows a university to avoid teaching controversial topics.

choices.label: E
choices.text: it eliminates the need for scholarships.

questions.explanation: Passage B allows divestment sometimes, with specified risks and standards.

questions.stem: In Passage A, the phrase “one lesson in classrooms and another through its portfolio” is used to suggest that

choices.label: A
choices.text: investment choices can conflict with a university's professed educational values.

choices.label: B
choices.text: students learn finance only by looking at portfolios.

choices.label: C
choices.text: classroom lessons are never connected to institutional behavior.

choices.label: D
choices.text: universities should invest only in textbook publishers.

choices.label: E
choices.text: all investment profits are educationally harmful.

questions.explanation: The phrase points to inconsistency between stated mission and investment behavior.

questions.stem: Which one of the following proposals would most likely be viewed more favorably by both authors than an automatic divestment rule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A policy requiring review of investments that conflict with the university mission, including public reasons and financial-risk analysis before any divestment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A rule requiring divestment whenever any campus group files a petition.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A policy forbidding the university from ever considering moral concerns.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A rule requiring all endowment assets to be kept in cash.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A policy allowing donors to secretly choose all investments.

questions.explanation: This combines Passage A's concern with mission and Passage B's concern with standards and financial risk.

sections.title: Logical Reasoning
sections.directions: Directions: Read each short passage and choose the answer that best responds to the question. Do not assume anything beyond what is stated unless the question asks you to do so.

questions.stimulus: In a regional policy review of a municipal program, only archival maps that are reviewed by counsel are available to outside auditors. The manager found a archival map that is reviewed by counsel, and therefore concluded that it is available to outside auditors.
questions.stem: The reasoning in the argument is vulnerable because it

choices.label: A
choices.text: takes for granted that the manager personally benefits from the conclusion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: confuses the order in which two events occurred.

choices.label: C
choices.text: treats a condition required for a status as though that condition were enough to guarantee the status.

choices.label: D
choices.text: relies on an analogy between two things that are not mentioned.

choices.label: E
choices.text: infers that something is common merely because it is possible.

questions.explanation: “Only P are Q” makes P necessary for Q, not sufficient for Q.

questions.stimulus: In a summer planning review of a regional program, after the service change, the average wait time at Argent Labs fell from 18 minutes to 11 minutes. The director concludes that every customer was served faster after the change.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the strongest criticism of the reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The director uses minutes rather than hours to report wait time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The argument assumes that customers dislike waiting.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The argument fails to show that the service change was popular with employees.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Averages can never be used to evaluate service quality.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A lower average wait time does not show that each individual customer waited less time.

questions.explanation: An average can fall even if some individual wait times stay the same or increase.

questions.stimulus: In a winter records review of a public program, after Clearwater Clinic simplified lab-result pages, portal logins rose. City officials therefore concluded that the change caused the improvement and that the same measure should be expanded to all comparable programs next year.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It assumes that a policy must be perfect in order to be worth considering.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It treats a before-and-after change as proof of causation without ruling out other causes or showing that the programs are comparable.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It rejects numerical evidence merely because the evidence is statistical.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It confuses the popularity of a proposal with its legality.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It concludes that a result is impossible because it has not yet occurred.

questions.explanation: The argument moves from a temporal association to causation and then to a broader recommendation without addressing alternatives or comparability.

questions.stimulus: In a annual safety review of a district program, a policy analyst argues that painted murals near bus stops caused the reported improvement because, in the first full year after the change, trash complaints decreased. The analyst notes that the measurement method was the same in both years.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some residents said they had not heard about the policy until months after it began.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The policy was less expensive to administer than officials had predicted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Several neighboring jurisdictions considered a similar policy but did not adopt it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No other major change affecting the measured result occurred in South Quay during the comparison period.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The analyst personally favored the policy before reviewing the data.

questions.explanation: Ruling out other major changes makes the policy a more plausible explanation for the improvement.

questions.stimulus: In a pilot-phase governance review of a clinic program, the director of the public garden workshops says the initiative was successful: after Hollis County held composting workshops, home composting registrations increased. The director concludes that the initiative itself produced the improvement.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: During the same period, registration was newly required for rebates, which would independently tend to produce the same reported result.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The initiative cost less than the director initially expected.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some residents were unaware that the initiative had begun.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The director had supported similar initiatives in other places.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The reported result was measured in percentages rather than raw numbers.

questions.explanation: An independent cause of the same result weakens the claim that the initiative itself produced the improvement.

questions.stimulus: In a regional policy review of a agency program, the committee should treat the survey dataset as included in the annual report, since it is filed before June 1. Every document that is filed before June 1 is normally processed quickly.
questions.stem: The argument depends on assuming which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No document that is not filed before June 1 is ever processed quickly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The committee has reviewed more survey datasets than any other kind of document.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Being processed quickly is enough, in this case, for the survey dataset to be treated as included in the annual report.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Documents treated as included in the annual report are always controversial.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The person who submitted the survey dataset expected it to be processed quickly.

questions.explanation: The premise establishes quick processing; the conclusion needs a bridge from quick processing to the special treatment recommended.

questions.stimulus: In a summer planning review of a court program, any budget worksheet that is accompanied by receipts is exempt from the usual fee. Any budget worksheet that is exempt from the usual fee must be logged by the records office. Galen's budget worksheet was not logged by the records office.
questions.stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Galen's budget worksheet was exempt from the usual fee.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No budget worksheet is logged unless it is accompanied by receipts.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every logged budget worksheet is accompanied by receipts.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Galen's budget worksheet was the only one not logged by the records office.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Galen's budget worksheet was not accompanied by receipts.

questions.explanation: The chain is prop -> secondary -> logged. Since the item was not logged, it cannot have had the original sufficient property.

questions.stimulus: In a winter records review of a nonprofit program, last quarter, a bakery used less flour. Nevertheless, it sold more loaves during the same quarter.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps resolve the apparent discrepancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The organization changed the color of its printed receipts.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It shifted toward smaller loaves with higher prices.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Managers expected the trend to be exactly the opposite.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The data were collected by the same employees who collected the previous quarter's data.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Several customers said they preferred the organization's old logo.

questions.explanation: The credited answer introduces a factor that allows the two apparently conflicting facts to be true at the same time.

questions.stimulus: In a annual safety review of a institutional program, the board defended its decision regarding automated benefit denials. It argued that the decision was limited to the specific harm at issue and left other activities unaffected. Therefore, the decision was justified.
questions.stem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps justify the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A rule is justified only if every person affected by it agrees with it in advance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An organization should never regulate activities that have any supporters.

choices.label: C
choices.text: When a decision has a cost, the decision is unjustified unless the cost is zero.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A public agency should disclose the basis for a decision whenever affected people cannot otherwise know why they were denied a benefit.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A decision is justified whenever it is easier to administer than any alternative.

questions.explanation: The principle connects a limited restriction aimed at a relevant harm to the conclusion that the decision is justified.

questions.stimulus: In a pilot-phase governance review of a campus program, some argue that the bridge should be replaced because it is old. But recent inspections show that its load-bearing parts remain sound. Hence age alone is not a good reason to replace the bridge.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the main conclusion of the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Age alone is not a good reason to replace the bridge.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The issue has attracted more public attention than it did in previous years.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The evidence cited by the argument is impossible to verify.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Several people disagree with the position defended in the argument.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The argument contains no recommendation about future legislation.

questions.explanation: The credited answer states the claim for which the other statements are offered as support.

questions.stimulus: In a regional policy review of a neighborhood program, all service contracts that are printed on blue paper are listed on the public website. This service contract is printed on blue paper. Therefore, this service contract is listed on the public website.
questions.stem: Which one of the following exhibits reasoning most similar to the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All archived reports are approved by the committee. This report is archived. Therefore, this report was approved by the committee.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some approved reports are archived. This report is approved. Therefore, this report might be archived.

choices.label: C
choices.text: All reports approved by the committee are archived. This report was approved by the committee. Therefore, this report is archived.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No archived report is missing. This report is missing. Therefore, this report is archived.

choices.label: E
choices.text: This report is archived only if it was approved. This report was approved. Therefore, it is archived.

questions.explanation: Both arguments instantiate a universal conditional: all P are Q; this is P; therefore this is Q.

questions.stimulus: In a summer planning review of a municipal program, the planning office concludes that the proposal should be adopted. The proposal would installed self-service kiosks. The office also says any proposal that would produce a measurable public benefit should be adopted.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if assumed, enables the conclusion to be properly drawn?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some proposals with measurable public benefits are unpopular at first.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No proposal should be adopted merely because it is inexpensive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The planning office has rejected proposals that lacked measurable public benefits.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A proposal can be adopted even if it has no measurable public benefit.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A proposal that would installed self-service kiosks would produce a measurable public benefit.

questions.explanation: The conclusion follows if the specific proposal falls within the class that the office says should be adopted.

questions.stimulus: In a winter records review of a regional program, a commentator says the improvement proves that released a public art map worked. The reply points out that a travel magazine featured the town, so the same improvement might have occurred even without the initiative.
questions.stem: The argument proceeds by

choices.label: A
choices.text: deducing a broad moral rule from a single legal requirement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: showing that the evidence offered for a causal claim is also compatible with a different explanation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: attacking the character of the commentator instead of addressing the evidence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: showing that a conclusion contradicts itself in formal terms.

choices.label: E
choices.text: appealing to popular support as the sole basis for truth.

questions.explanation: The reply weakens the causal interpretation by presenting an alternative explanation for the same evidence.

questions.stimulus: In a annual safety review of a public program, no training certificate that is signed by the director is valid for two years. Every document in Folder K is signed by the director. Mira's document is in Folder K.
questions.stem: Which one of the following follows logically from the statements above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mira's document is the only document in Folder K.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every training certificate that is not valid for two years is in Folder K.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No document outside Folder K is signed by the director.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mira's document is not valid for two years.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some documents in Folder K are valid for two years.

questions.explanation: Folder K documents have the stated property, and no document with that property has the secondary status.

questions.stimulus: In a pilot-phase governance review of a district program, only compliance letters that are printed on blue paper are listed on the public website. The manager found a compliance letter that is printed on blue paper, and therefore concluded that it is listed on the public website.
questions.stem: The reasoning in the argument is vulnerable because it

choices.label: A
choices.text: treats a condition required for a status as though that condition were enough to guarantee the status.

choices.label: B
choices.text: takes for granted that the manager personally benefits from the conclusion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: confuses the order in which two events occurred.

choices.label: D
choices.text: relies on an analogy between two things that are not mentioned.

choices.label: E
choices.text: infers that something is common merely because it is possible.

questions.explanation: “Only P are Q” makes P necessary for Q, not sufficient for Q.

questions.stimulus: In a regional policy review of a clinic program, after the service change, the average wait time at Pelican Sound fell from 18 minutes to 11 minutes. The director concludes that every customer was served faster after the change.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the strongest criticism of the reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The director uses minutes rather than hours to report wait time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The argument assumes that customers dislike waiting.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A lower average wait time does not show that each individual customer waited less time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The argument fails to show that the service change was popular with employees.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Averages can never be used to evaluate service quality.

questions.explanation: An average can fall even if some individual wait times stay the same or increase.

questions.stimulus: In a summer planning review of a agency program, after Graystone Dorms installed high-efficiency washers, water bills decreased. City officials therefore concluded that the change caused the improvement and that the same measure should be expanded to all comparable programs next year.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It assumes that a policy must be perfect in order to be worth considering.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It rejects numerical evidence merely because the evidence is statistical.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It confuses the popularity of a proposal with its legality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It concludes that a result is impossible because it has not yet occurred.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It treats a before-and-after change as proof of causation without ruling out other causes or showing that the programs are comparable.

questions.explanation: The argument moves from a temporal association to causation and then to a broader recommendation without addressing alternatives or comparability.

questions.stimulus: In a winter records review of a court program, a policy analyst argues that created a mediation desk caused the reported improvement because, in the first full year after the change, settlements increased. The analyst notes that the measurement method was the same in both years.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some residents said they had not heard about the policy until months after it began.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No other major change affecting the measured result occurred in Alder Housing Court during the comparison period.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The policy was less expensive to administer than officials had predicted.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Several neighboring jurisdictions considered a similar policy but did not adopt it.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The analyst personally favored the policy before reviewing the data.

questions.explanation: Ruling out other major changes makes the policy a more plausible explanation for the improvement.

questions.stimulus: In a annual safety review of a nonprofit program, the director of the snow-plow routing software says the initiative was successful: after Northfield used new routing software, streets were cleared faster. The director concludes that the initiative itself produced the improvement.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The initiative cost less than the director initially expected.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some residents were unaware that the initiative had begun.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The director had supported similar initiatives in other places.

choices.label: D
choices.text: During the same period, the winter had fewer snowstorms, which would independently tend to produce the same reported result.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The reported result was measured in percentages rather than raw numbers.

questions.explanation: An independent cause of the same result weakens the claim that the initiative itself produced the improvement.

questions.stimulus: In a pilot-phase governance review of a institutional program, the committee should treat the permit file as flagged for follow-up, since it is approved unanimously. Every document that is approved unanimously is normally processed quickly.
questions.stem: The argument depends on assuming which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Being processed quickly is enough, in this case, for the permit file to be treated as flagged for follow-up.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No document that is not approved unanimously is ever processed quickly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The committee has reviewed more permit files than any other kind of document.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Documents treated as flagged for follow-up are always controversial.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The person who submitted the permit file expected it to be processed quickly.

questions.explanation: The premise establishes quick processing; the conclusion needs a bridge from quick processing to the special treatment recommended.

questions.stimulus: In a regional policy review of a campus program, any lab notebook that is filed before June 1 is kept in the climate-controlled room. Any lab notebook that is kept in the climate-controlled room must be logged by the records office. Uma's lab notebook was not logged by the records office.
questions.stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Uma's lab notebook was kept in the climate-controlled room.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No lab notebook is logged unless it is filed before June 1.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Uma's lab notebook was not filed before June 1.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every logged lab notebook is filed before June 1.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Uma's lab notebook was the only one not logged by the records office.

questions.explanation: The chain is prop -> secondary -> logged. Since the item was not logged, it cannot have had the original sufficient property.

questions.stimulus: In a summer planning review of a neighborhood program, last quarter, a museum reduced weekday hours. Nevertheless, weekday admissions increased during the same quarter.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps resolve the apparent discrepancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The organization changed the color of its printed receipts.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Managers expected the trend to be exactly the opposite.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The data were collected by the same employees who collected the previous quarter's data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Several customers said they preferred the organization's old logo.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A popular temporary exhibit opened during the reduced hours.

questions.explanation: The credited answer introduces a factor that allows the two apparently conflicting facts to be true at the same time.

questions.stimulus: In a winter records review of a municipal program, the board defended its decision regarding chemistry labs during exam week. It argued that the decision was limited to the specific harm at issue and left other activities unaffected. Therefore, the decision was justified.
questions.stem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps justify the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A rule is justified only if every person affected by it agrees with it in advance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A university may reserve scarce laboratory time for courses that require equipment use to meet their stated learning goals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An organization should never regulate activities that have any supporters.

choices.label: D
choices.text: When a decision has a cost, the decision is unjustified unless the cost is zero.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A decision is justified whenever it is easier to administer than any alternative.

questions.explanation: The principle connects a limited restriction aimed at a relevant harm to the conclusion that the decision is justified.

questions.stimulus: In a annual safety review of a regional program, several reviewers praised the film's historical accuracy. Yet the film changes the chronology of the central trial and invents two major speeches. Thus, the reviewers' praise should not be taken as proof that the film is reliable history.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the main conclusion of the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The issue has attracted more public attention than it did in previous years.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The evidence cited by the argument is impossible to verify.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Several people disagree with the position defended in the argument.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The reviewers' praise should not be taken as proof that the film is reliable history.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The argument contains no recommendation about future legislation.

questions.explanation: The credited answer states the claim for which the other statements are offered as support.

questions.stimulus: In a pilot-phase governance review of a public program, all inspection logs that are signed by the director are eligible for fast review. This inspection log is signed by the director. Therefore, this inspection log is eligible for fast review.
questions.stem: Which one of the following exhibits reasoning most similar to the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All reports approved by the committee are archived. This report was approved by the committee. Therefore, this report is archived.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All archived reports are approved by the committee. This report is archived. Therefore, this report was approved by the committee.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some approved reports are archived. This report is approved. Therefore, this report might be archived.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No archived report is missing. This report is missing. Therefore, this report is archived.

choices.label: E
choices.text: This report is archived only if it was approved. This report was approved. Therefore, it is archived.

sections.title: Logical Reasoning
sections.directions: Directions: Read each short passage and choose the answer that best responds to the question. Do not assume anything beyond what is stated unless the question asks you to do so.

questions.stimulus: In a midyear community review of a district program, the planning office concludes that the proposal should be adopted. The proposal would added electric ferries during morning commute. The office also says any proposal that would produce a measurable public benefit should be adopted.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if assumed, enables the conclusion to be properly drawn?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some proposals with measurable public benefits are unpopular at first.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No proposal should be adopted merely because it is inexpensive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A proposal that would added electric ferries during morning commute would produce a measurable public benefit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The planning office has rejected proposals that lacked measurable public benefits.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A proposal can be adopted even if it has no measurable public benefit.

questions.explanation: The conclusion follows if the specific proposal falls within the class that the office says should be adopted.

questions.stimulus: In a biennial performance review of a clinic program, a commentator says the improvement proves that kept two branches open until 10 p.m. worked. The reply points out that a new state rule required card updates, so the same improvement might have occurred even without the initiative.
questions.stem: The argument proceeds by

choices.label: A
choices.text: deducing a broad moral rule from a single legal requirement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: attacking the character of the commentator instead of addressing the evidence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: showing that a conclusion contradicts itself in formal terms.

choices.label: D
choices.text: appealing to popular support as the sole basis for truth.

choices.label: E
choices.text: showing that the evidence offered for a causal claim is also compatible with a different explanation.

questions.explanation: The reply weakens the causal interpretation by presenting an alternative explanation for the same evidence.

questions.stimulus: In a post-implementation compliance review of a agency program, no budget worksheet that is submitted electronically is sent to the oversight board. Every document in Folder K is submitted electronically. Mira's document is in Folder K.
questions.stem: Which one of the following follows logically from the statements above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mira's document is the only document in Folder K.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mira's document is not sent to the oversight board.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every budget worksheet that is not sent to the oversight board is in Folder K.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No document outside Folder K is submitted electronically.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some documents in Folder K are sent to the oversight board.

questions.explanation: Folder K documents have the stated property, and no document with that property has the secondary status.

questions.stimulus: In a spring equity review of a court program, only audit reports that are signed by the director are eligible for fast review. The manager found a audit report that is signed by the director, and therefore concluded that it is eligible for fast review.
questions.stem: The reasoning in the argument is vulnerable because it

choices.label: A
choices.text: takes for granted that the manager personally benefits from the conclusion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: confuses the order in which two events occurred.

choices.label: C
choices.text: relies on an analogy between two things that are not mentioned.

choices.label: D
choices.text: treats a condition required for a status as though that condition were enough to guarantee the status.

choices.label: E
choices.text: infers that something is common merely because it is possible.

questions.explanation: “Only P are Q” makes P necessary for Q, not sufficient for Q.

questions.stimulus: In a autumn research review of a nonprofit program, after the service change, the average wait time at North Pier Museum fell from 18 minutes to 11 minutes. The director concludes that every customer was served faster after the change.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the strongest criticism of the reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A lower average wait time does not show that each individual customer waited less time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The director uses minutes rather than hours to report wait time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The argument assumes that customers dislike waiting.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The argument fails to show that the service change was popular with employees.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Averages can never be used to evaluate service quality.

questions.explanation: An average can fall even if some individual wait times stay the same or increase.

questions.stimulus: In a midyear community review of a institutional program, after Old Quarry installed brighter LED streetlights downtown, nighttime shop visits rose. City officials therefore concluded that the change caused the improvement and that the same measure should be expanded to all comparable programs next year.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It assumes that a policy must be perfect in order to be worth considering.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It rejects numerical evidence merely because the evidence is statistical.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It treats a before-and-after change as proof of causation without ruling out other causes or showing that the programs are comparable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It confuses the popularity of a proposal with its legality.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It concludes that a result is impossible because it has not yet occurred.

questions.explanation: The argument moves from a temporal association to causation and then to a broader recommendation without addressing alternatives or comparability.

questions.stimulus: In a biennial performance review of a campus program, a policy analyst argues that required restaurants to separate food waste caused the reported improvement because, in the first full year after the change, landfill trash tonnage fell. questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.label: D
choices.text: The analyst personally favored the policy before reviewing the data.

choices.label: E
choices.text: No other major change affecting the measured result occurred in Larchmont during the comparison period.

questions.explanation: Ruling out other major changes makes the policy a more plausible explanation for the improvement.

sections.title: Reading Comprehension
sections.directions: Directions: Each set of questions is based on a single passage or a pair of related passages. Answer each question on the basis of what is stated or implied in the passage.

passages.title: Restorative Justice in Juvenile Courts
passages.text:
Restorative justice programs in juvenile courts ask young offenders to confront the harm caused by their conduct and, where appropriate, to repair that harm through apologies, service, or restitution. Their supporters often contrast this approach with punishment that isolates the young person from the community. But the contrast can be overstated. A restorative conference ordered by a court is still backed by state authority, and that authority can shape what participants feel free to say.

This does not mean restorative programs are merely punishment in softer language. Many victims report that having a structured opportunity to ask questions matters more than the formal sentence. Many youths, too, respond differently when they must address a person harmed by their act rather than an abstract rule. The promise of restoration lies in this relational focus.

The risk is that courts may adopt restorative language while keeping the old incentives. If success is measured only by the number of completed conferences, officials may pressure victims to participate or push youths toward scripted remorse. A program can then appear humane while reproducing coercion in a new form.

A better measure of success would consider process: whether participation was meaningfully voluntary for victims, whether youths understood the obligations they accepted, and whether agreements addressed actual harms rather than merely producing paperwork. Restorative justice is valuable not because it is outside law, but because it can make law more responsive when its procedures are carefully designed.

passages.title: Urban Tree Canopies and Heat
passages.text:
Urban tree canopies lower neighborhood temperatures by shading pavement and moving water from soil to air through transpiration. Because heat waves now pose serious health risks, cities often treat tree planting as a climate-adaptation strategy. Maps of canopy cover can also reveal a familiar inequity: wealthier neighborhoods often have more shade than lower-income neighborhoods exposed to larger expanses of asphalt.

Planting trees, however, is not as simple as filling blank spaces on a map. Young trees provide little shade, require years of watering, and may die if planted in compacted soil or in narrow pits surrounded by utilities. In some neighborhoods, residents may also distrust planting campaigns associated with rising rents or with outside beautification projects that ignore local needs.

Effective canopy policy therefore combines biology with governance. Cities must select species suited to heat, pests, and available rooting space. They must also fund maintenance after the ceremonial planting day and involve residents in decisions about location and care. Otherwise a city can count thousands of new trees while gaining little durable shade.

The best canopy programs treat trees as infrastructure rather than decoration. A bridge budget would not pay only for ribbon-cutting and ignore inspections; tree budgets likewise need watering, pruning, replacement, and community stewardship. The measure of success should not be seedlings planted but shade that survives where heat risk is greatest.

passages.title: Visible Repair in Museum Conservation
passages.text:
For much of the twentieth century, museum conservation aimed to make repairs disappear. A cracked ceramic vessel or torn textile was restored so that viewers could imagine an undamaged original. This approach had a democratic appeal: it allowed nonexpert visitors to encounter the object without being distracted by technical scars. But invisible repair also carried a fiction, suggesting that objects pass through history untouched.

In recent decades, some conservators have defended visible repair. They argue that cracks, patches, and joins can be part of an object's biography. A repaired bowl may tell not only of the workshop that made it but also of the household that valued it enough to mend it, the trade routes that moved repair materials, or the museum choices that stabilized it. Visibility can therefore expand, rather than diminish, historical meaning.

Visible repair is not a license for neglect or spectacle. A repair that overwhelms the object can substitute the conservator's signature for the object's own evidence. Nor should every repaired area be left conspicuous. Some objects were designed for ritual or visual effects that excessive contrast would distort. Conservation remains an interpretive practice, balancing material honesty with the object's function and context.

The debate is best understood not as a choice between truth and beauty but as a question about which truths and which beauties a museum chooses to foreground. Invisible repair can preserve a viewer's sense of original form; visible repair can preserve a record of use, damage, and care. The responsible conservator makes that choice explicit.

passages.title: Comparative Reading - Remote Work and Cities
passages.text:
Passage A: Remote work has been blamed for weakening downtowns, but the diagnosis is too narrow. Many central business districts were fragile before office workers stayed home: they depended on five-day-a-week commuter traffic, separated offices from housing, and offered little reason to visit after business hours. Remote work exposed this brittleness. The answer is not to force workers back into old patterns but to diversify downtown land use, convert obsolete offices to housing where feasible, and make public spaces attractive beyond lunch hour.

Passage B: The enthusiasm for remote work overlooks costs that are unevenly distributed. Professional employees may enjoy flexibility, but restaurants, cleaners, transit systems, and small retailers built around office density cannot pivot overnight. A city is an ecosystem of interdependence, and the disappearance of daily commuters can damage workers who never had the option to work from home. Policy should encourage a partial return to offices, not because the old commute was ideal, but because abrupt withdrawal imposes public costs that individual employers may ignore.
