choices.text: has any connection to harm repair.

questions.explanation: The author says restorative justice can improve law without pretending to be beyond law.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward restorative justice is best described as

choices.label: A
choices.text: guardedly favorable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: wholly dismissive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: uncritically celebratory.

choices.label: D
choices.text: indifferent.

choices.label: E
choices.text: nostalgic.

questions.explanation: The author sees promise but repeatedly warns about design and coercion.

questions.stem: The passage is primarily concerned with

choices.label: A
choices.text: arguing that equitable tree-canopy policy requires long-term maintenance and community governance, not merely planting targets.

choices.label: B
choices.text: showing that trees cannot lower urban temperatures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: explaining why cities should plant only one species of tree.

choices.label: D
choices.text: denying that heat waves pose health risks.

choices.label: E
choices.text: claiming that wealthier neighborhoods have too little shade.

questions.explanation: The author emphasizes durable shade, maintenance, and resident involvement.

questions.stem: According to the passage, maps of canopy cover can reveal

choices.label: A
choices.text: unequal distribution of shade across neighborhoods.

choices.label: B
choices.text: that all neighborhoods have identical asphalt coverage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the exact age of every city tree.

choices.label: D
choices.text: that tree planting always raises rents.

choices.label: E
choices.text: which bridges require inspection.

questions.explanation: The first paragraph says canopy maps can reveal inequity.

questions.stem: The author mentions compacted soil and narrow pits primarily to show that

choices.label: A
choices.text: biological and physical conditions can prevent new trees from becoming durable shade.

choices.label: B
choices.text: residents never want new trees.

choices.label: C
choices.text: shade appears immediately after planting.

choices.label: D
choices.text: utilities are unrelated to planting decisions.

choices.label: E
choices.text: asphalt improves tree survival.

questions.explanation: These are practical conditions that can cause planting efforts to fail.

questions.stem: Which one of the following would the author most likely criticize?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A city announces success based on seedlings planted but allocates no money for watering or replacement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A city chooses species suited to local heat and pests.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A city asks residents where shade is most needed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A city tracks survival and canopy growth over time.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A city replaces dead trees after extreme weather.

questions.explanation: The passage criticizes counting plantings without durable maintenance.

questions.stem: The analogy to a bridge budget is used to support the claim that trees

choices.label: A
choices.text: require continuing maintenance as infrastructure does.

choices.label: B
choices.text: should be built from concrete.

choices.label: C
choices.text: are less useful than bridges during heat waves.

choices.label: D
choices.text: should never be planted near roads.

choices.label: E
choices.text: cost exactly the same amount as bridges.

questions.explanation: The bridge analogy illustrates ongoing inspection and maintenance.

questions.stem: The phrase “ceremonial planting day” most nearly refers to

choices.label: A
choices.text: a public launch event that may receive attention while later maintenance is neglected.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a scientific method for measuring transpiration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the legal date on which trees become public property.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the day a bridge is inspected.

choices.label: E
choices.text: a holiday celebrated only in shaded neighborhoods.

questions.explanation: The author contrasts the public planting event with ongoing care.

questions.stem: The author's main point is that museum repair should

choices.label: A
choices.text: be understood as an interpretive choice that may responsibly be visible or invisible depending on context.

choices.label: B
choices.text: always hide all evidence of damage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: always make repairs more noticeable than the original object.

choices.label: D
choices.text: avoid considering the object's function.

choices.label: E
choices.text: be performed only on ceramics.

questions.explanation: The author rejects an absolute rule and stresses explicit interpretive choice.

questions.stem: According to the passage, invisible repair can be misleading because it may suggest that objects

choices.label: A
choices.text: have passed through history untouched.

choices.label: B
choices.text: were never intended to be viewed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: are less beautiful than repaired objects.

choices.label: D
choices.text: cannot be understood by nonexperts.

choices.label: E
choices.text: were all made in the same workshop.

questions.explanation: The first paragraph says invisible repair carries that fiction.

questions.stem: The author uses the example of a repaired bowl to show that visible repair can reveal

choices.label: A
choices.text: an object's later history of use, value, movement, and museum care.

choices.label: B
choices.text: that household objects lack artistic value.

choices.label: C
choices.text: that workshops never matter to interpretation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: that repair materials are always local.

choices.label: E
choices.text: that museums should display only broken objects.

questions.explanation: The bowl example illustrates the expanded biography of the object.

questions.stem: Which one of the following would the author most likely reject?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A repair should be made conspicuous in every case because visibility is always more truthful.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A repaired area may sometimes be hidden to preserve a ritual effect.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conservators should consider how repair changes historical meaning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Visible joins can help viewers understand an object's biography.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Museums should be explicit about conservation choices.

questions.explanation: The third paragraph warns against making every repaired area conspicuous.

questions.stem: The phrase “the conservator's signature” refers to a repair that

choices.label: A
choices.text: draws attention to the conservator's intervention more than to the object's evidence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: records the conservator's name on a wall label.

choices.label: C
choices.text: perfectly preserves original form.

choices.label: D
choices.text: uses only historically authentic materials.

choices.label: E
choices.text: makes the object impossible to display.

questions.explanation: The author warns against repairs that overwhelm the object.

questions.stem: The final paragraph functions primarily to

choices.label: A
choices.text: reframe the debate as a choice among values rather than as a simple opposition.

choices.label: B
choices.text: announce that beauty should always defeat truth.

choices.label: C
choices.text: summarize the chemical methods used in conservation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: claim that invisible repair has no benefits.

choices.label: E
choices.text: argue that museums should stop conserving objects.

questions.explanation: The final paragraph recasts the debate in terms of which truths and beauties to foreground.

questions.stem: Which one of the following best matches the author's view of conservation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is a practice involving judgment about material evidence, function, context, and viewer experience.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a mechanical process with one correct answer for every object.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It should be kept secret from museum visitors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is valuable only when it makes objects appear new.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It should never consider historical use.

questions.explanation: The passage repeatedly presents conservation as interpretive judgment.

questions.stem: Both passages discuss remote work in relation to

choices.label: A
choices.text: the economic and spatial health of downtown areas.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the history of factory automation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the design of private homes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: whether all jobs can be done online.

choices.label: E
choices.text: the legal definition of overtime.

questions.explanation: Both passages connect remote work to downtown economies and land use.

questions.stem: Passage A differs from Passage B in that Passage A emphasizes

choices.label: A
choices.text: preexisting weaknesses in downtown design that remote work revealed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the need to force all workers back five days a week.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the impossibility of converting offices to housing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the idea that restaurants are unaffected by commuter patterns.

choices.label: E
choices.text: the superiority of suburban malls.

questions.explanation: Passage A says remote work exposed a brittle preexisting downtown model.

questions.stem: The author of Passage B would most likely criticize Passage A for giving insufficient attention to

choices.label: A
choices.text: workers and businesses dependent on daily office density during the transition.

choices.label: B
choices.text: whether public spaces should exist downtown.

choices.label: C
choices.text: whether housing can ever be built in cities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: whether professionals enjoy flexibility.

choices.label: E
choices.text: the fact that some offices are obsolete.

questions.explanation: Passage B emphasizes uneven costs for service workers and small businesses.

questions.stem: Which one of the following proposals would likely receive support from both authors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A plan to convert some offices to housing while also providing transition support for downtown service workers and transit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A law requiring every office worker to commute five days a week forever.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A policy abandoning downtown transit because remote work exists.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A ban on housing in central business districts.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A plan to ignore small retailers until commuter traffic returns on its own.

questions.explanation: This combines diversification from Passage A with concern for transition costs from Passage B.

questions.stem: In Passage A, “brittleness” refers to

choices.label: A
choices.text: a downtown model overly dependent on a narrow pattern of commuter office use.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the physical weakness of office windows.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the speed of home internet connections.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the refusal of restaurants to serve lunch.

choices.label: E
choices.text: a legal defect in zoning maps.

questions.explanation: Passage A defines brittleness through overdependence on commuter traffic and single-use zoning.

questions.stem: Passage B uses the phrase “ecosystem of interdependence” to stress that

choices.label: A
choices.text: changes benefiting some workers can harm other connected workers and services.

choices.label: B
choices.text: downtowns are natural forests.

choices.label: C
choices.text: remote work prevents all economic activity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: professional employees never depend on cities.

choices.label: E
choices.text: employers always account for public costs.

questions.explanation: The phrase highlights linked economic effects.

questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes the relationship between the passages?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Passage A treats remote work as a chance to redesign downtowns; Passage B warns that the transition creates external costs for dependent workers and services.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Passage A and Passage B both claim that remote work has no effect on cities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Passage A focuses on individual productivity while Passage B focuses on computer hardware.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Passage B proves that Passage A supports suburbanization.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Passage A rejects urban policy while Passage B rejects economic analysis.

questions.explanation: The passages frame different policy emphases around the same shift.

sections.title: Logical Reasoning
sections.directions: Directions: Read each short passage and choose the answer that best responds to the question. Do not assume anything beyond what is stated unless the question asks you to do so.

questions.stimulus: In a pilot-phase research review of a clinic program, no permit file that is submitted electronically is sent to the oversight board. Every document in Folder K is submitted electronically. Mira's document is in Folder K.
questions.stem: Which one of the following follows logically from the statements above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mira's document is the only document in Folder K.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every permit file that is not sent to the oversight board is in Folder K.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No document outside Folder K is submitted electronically.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Some documents in Folder K are sent to the oversight board.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mira's document is not sent to the oversight board.

questions.explanation: Folder K documents have the stated property, and no document with that property has the secondary status.

questions.stimulus: In a regional community review of a agency program, only inspection logs that are signed by the director are eligible for fast review. The manager found a inspection log that is signed by the director, and therefore concluded that it is eligible for fast review.
questions.stem: The reasoning in the argument is vulnerable because it

choices.label: A
choices.text: takes for granted that the manager personally benefits from the conclusion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: treats a condition required for a status as though that condition were enough to guarantee the status.

choices.label: C
choices.text: confuses the order in which two events occurred.

choices.label: D
choices.text: relies on an analogy between two things that are not mentioned.

choices.label: E
choices.text: infers that something is common merely because it is possible.

questions.explanation: “Only P are Q” makes P necessary for Q, not sufficient for Q.

questions.stimulus: In a summer performance review of a court program, after the service change, the average wait time at Meridian College fell from 18 minutes to 11 minutes. The director concludes that every customer was served faster after the change.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the strongest criticism of the reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The director uses minutes rather than hours to report wait time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The argument assumes that customers dislike waiting.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The argument fails to show that the service change was popular with employees.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A lower average wait time does not show that each individual customer waited less time.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Averages can never be used to evaluate service quality.

questions.explanation: An average can fall even if some individual wait times stay the same or increase.

questions.stimulus: In a winter compliance review of a nonprofit program, after Willow Bend gave produce vouchers to low-income residents, market sales increased. City officials therefore concluded that the change caused the improvement and that the same measure should be expanded to all comparable programs next year.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It treats a before-and-after change as proof of causation without ruling out other causes or showing that the programs are comparable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It assumes that a policy must be perfect in order to be worth considering.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It rejects numerical evidence merely because the evidence is statistical.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It confuses the popularity of a proposal with its legality.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It concludes that a result is impossible because it has not yet occurred.

questions.explanation: The argument moves from a temporal association to causation and then to a broader recommendation without addressing alternatives or comparability.

questions.stimulus: In a annual equity review of a institutional program, a policy analyst argues that painted protected lanes on four streets caused the reported improvement because, in the first full year after the change, bicycle commuting increased. The analyst notes that the measurement method was the same in both years.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some residents said they had not heard about the policy until months after it began.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The policy was less expensive to administer than officials had predicted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No other major change affecting the measured result occurred in Cedar Falls during the comparison period.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Several neighboring jurisdictions considered a similar policy but did not adopt it.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The analyst personally favored the policy before reviewing the data.

questions.explanation: Ruling out other major changes makes the policy a more plausible explanation for the improvement.

questions.stimulus: In a pilot-phase research review of a campus program, the director of the water audit program says the initiative was successful: after Sunvale offered free home water audits, household water use declined. The director concludes that the initiative itself produced the improvement.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The initiative cost less than the director initially expected.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some residents were unaware that the initiative had begun.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The director had supported similar initiatives in other places.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The reported result was measured in percentages rather than raw numbers.

choices.label: E
choices.text: During the same period, rainfall was unusually high that year, which would independently tend to produce the same reported result.

questions.explanation: An independent cause of the same result weakens the claim that the initiative itself produced the improvement.

questions.stimulus: In a regional community review of a neighborhood program, the committee should treat the lab notebook as treated as final, since it is stored in the main archive. Every document that is stored in the main archive is normally processed quickly.
questions.stem: The argument depends on assuming which one of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No document that is not stored in the main archive is ever processed quickly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Being processed quickly is enough, in this case, for the lab notebook to be treated as treated as final.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The committee has reviewed more lab notebooks than any other kind of document.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Documents treated as treated as final are always controversial.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The person who submitted the lab notebook expected it to be processed quickly.

questions.explanation: The premise establishes quick processing; the conclusion needs a bridge from quick processing to the special treatment recommended.

questions.stimulus: In a summer performance review of a municipal program, any warranty card that is approved unanimously is available to outside auditors. Any warranty card that is available to outside auditors must be logged by the records office. Kira's warranty card was not logged by the records office.
questions.stem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following must be true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Kira's warranty card was available to outside auditors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No warranty card is logged unless it is approved unanimously.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every logged warranty card is approved unanimously.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Kira's warranty card was not approved unanimously.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Kira's warranty card was the only one not logged by the records office.

questions.explanation: The chain is prop -> secondary -> logged. Since the item was not logged, it cannot have had the original sufficient property.

questions.stimulus: In a winter compliance review of a regional program, last quarter, a city printed fewer parking tickets. Nevertheless, parking fine revenue increased during the same quarter.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps resolve the apparent discrepancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The city increased the fine for the most common violation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The organization changed the color of its printed receipts.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Managers expected the trend to be exactly the opposite.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The data were collected by the same employees who collected the previous quarter's data.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Several customers said they preferred the organization's old logo.

questions.explanation: The credited answer introduces a factor that allows the two apparently conflicting facts to be true at the same time.

questions.stimulus: In a annual equity review of a public program, the board defended its decision regarding a robotics club occupying every lab after school. It argued that the decision was limited to the specific harm at issue and left other activities unaffected. Therefore, the decision was justified.
questions.stem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most helps justify the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A rule is justified only if every person affected by it agrees with it in advance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An organization should never regulate activities that have any supporters.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A school may limit a student club activity when the activity predictably prevents other students from using shared facilities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: When a decision has a cost, the decision is unjustified unless the cost is zero.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A decision is justified whenever it is easier to administer than any alternative.

questions.explanation: The principle connects a limited restriction aimed at a relevant harm to the conclusion that the decision is justified.

questions.stimulus: In a pilot-phase research review of a district program, several reviewers praised the film's historical accuracy. Yet the film changes the chronology of the central trial and invents two major speeches. Thus, the reviewers' praise should not be taken as proof that the film is reliable history.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the main conclusion of the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The issue has attracted more public attention than it did in previous years.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The evidence cited by the argument is impossible to verify.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Several people disagree with the position defended in the argument.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The argument contains no recommendation about future legislation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The reviewers' praise should not be taken as proof that the film is reliable history.

questions.explanation: The credited answer states the claim for which the other statements are offered as support.

questions.stimulus: In a regional community review of a clinic program, all committee memos that are submitted electronically are exempt from the usual fee. This committee memo is submitted electronically. Therefore, this committee memo is exempt from the usual fee.
questions.stem: Which one of the following exhibits reasoning most similar to the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All archived reports are approved by the committee. This report is archived. Therefore, this report was approved by the committee.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All reports approved by the committee are archived. This report was approved by the committee. Therefore, this report is archived.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Some approved reports are archived. This report is approved. Therefore, this report might be archived.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No archived report is missing. This report is missing. Therefore, this report is archived.

choices.label: E
choices.text: This report is archived only if it was approved. This report was approved. Therefore, it is archived.

questions.explanation: Both arguments instantiate a universal conditional: all P are Q; this is P; therefore this is Q.

questions.stimulus: In a summer performance review of a agency program, the planning office concludes that the proposal should be adopted. The proposal would introduced monthly safety drills. The office also says any proposal that would produce a measurable public benefit should be adopted.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if assumed, enables the conclusion to be properly drawn?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some proposals with measurable public benefits are unpopular at first.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No proposal should be adopted merely because it is inexpensive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The planning office has rejected proposals that lacked measurable public benefits.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A proposal that would introduced monthly safety drills would produce a measurable public benefit.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A proposal can be adopted even if it has no measurable public benefit.

questions.explanation: The conclusion follows if the specific proposal falls within the class that the office says should be adopted.

questions.stimulus: In a winter compliance review of a court program, a commentator says the improvement proves that sent daily email summaries worked. The reply points out that a local election drove unusual interest, so the same improvement might have occurred even without the initiative.
questions.stem: The argument proceeds by

choices.label: A
choices.text: showing that the evidence offered for a causal claim is also compatible with a different explanation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: deducing a broad moral rule from a single legal requirement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: attacking the character of the commentator instead of addressing the evidence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: showing that a conclusion contradicts itself in formal terms.

choices.label: E
choices.text: appealing to popular support as the sole basis for truth.

questions.explanation: The reply weakens the causal interpretation by presenting an alternative explanation for the same evidence.

questions.stimulus: In a annual equity review of a nonprofit program, no pilot license that is entered in the public ledger is included in the annual report. Every document in Folder K is entered in the public ledger. Mira's document is in Folder K.
questions.stem: Which one of the following follows logically from the statements above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mira's document is the only document in Folder K.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every pilot license that is not included in the annual report is in Folder K.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mira's document is not included in the annual report.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No document outside Folder K is entered in the public ledger.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Some documents in Folder K are included in the annual report.

questions.explanation: Folder K documents have the stated property, and no document with that property has the secondary status.

questions.stimulus: In a pilot-phase research review of a institutional program, only public notices that are submitted electronically are exempt from the usual fee. The manager found a public notice that is submitted electronically, and therefore concluded that it is exempt from the usual fee.
questions.stem: The reasoning in the argument is vulnerable because it

choices.label: A
choices.text: takes for granted that the manager personally benefits from the conclusion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: confuses the order in which two events occurred.

choices.label: C
choices.text: relies on an analogy between two things that are not mentioned.

choices.label: D
choices.text: infers that something is common merely because it is possible.

choices.label: E
choices.text: treats a condition required for a status as though that condition were enough to guarantee the status.

questions.explanation: “Only P are Q” makes P necessary for Q, not sufficient for Q.

questions.stimulus: In a regional community review of a campus program, after the service change, the average wait time at Maple Junction fell from 18 minutes to 11 minutes. The director concludes that every customer was served faster after the change.
questions.stem: Which one of the following is the strongest criticism of the reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The director uses minutes rather than hours to report wait time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A lower average wait time does not show that each individual customer waited less time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The argument assumes that customers dislike waiting.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The argument fails to show that the service change was popular with employees.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Averages can never be used to evaluate service quality.

questions.explanation: An average can fall even if some individual wait times stay the same or increase.

questions.stimulus: In a summer performance review of a neighborhood program, after Calder City put building permits online, permit processing time declined. City officials therefore concluded that the change caused the improvement and that the same measure should be expanded to all comparable programs next year.
questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It assumes that a policy must be perfect in order to be worth considering.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It rejects numerical evidence merely because the evidence is statistical.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It confuses the popularity of a proposal with its legality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It treats a before-and-after change as proof of causation without ruling out other causes or showing that the programs are comparable.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It concludes that a result is impossible because it has not yet occurred.

questions.explanation: The argument moves from a temporal association to causation and then to a broader recommendation without addressing alternatives or comparability.

questions.stimulus: In a winter compliance review of a municipal program, a policy analyst argues that reduced catch quotas for two seasons caused the reported improvement because, in the first full year after the change, cod counts increased. The analyst notes that the measurement method was the same in both years.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No other major change affecting the measured result occurred in Northbank Fishery during the comparison period.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some residents said they had not heard about the policy until months after it began.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The policy was less expensive to administer than officials had predicted.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Several neighboring jurisdictions considered a similar policy but did not adopt it.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The analyst personally favored the policy before reviewing the data.

questions.explanation: Ruling out other major changes makes the policy a more plausible explanation for the improvement.

questions.stimulus: In a winter compliance review of a agency program, the board defended its decision regarding a study based on destroyed tissue samples. sections.title: Logical Reasoning
sections.directions: Directions: Read each short passage and choose the answer that best responds to the question. Do not assume anything beyond what is stated unless the question asks you to do so.

questions.stimulus: In a autumn operations review of a nonprofit program, all shipping manifests that are entered in the public ledger are kept in the climate-controlled room. This shipping manifest is entered in the public ledger. Therefore, this shipping manifest is kept in the climate-controlled room.
questions.stem: Which one of the following exhibits reasoning most similar to the reasoning above?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All archived reports are approved by the committee. This report is archived. Therefore, this report was approved by the committee.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Some approved reports are archived. This report is approved. Therefore, this report might be archived.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No archived report is missing. This report is missing. Therefore, this report is archived.

choices.label: D
choices.text: This report is archived only if it was approved. This report was approved. Therefore, it is archived.

choices.label: E
choices.text: All reports approved by the committee are archived. This report was approved by the committee. Therefore, this report is archived.

questions.explanation: Both arguments instantiate a universal conditional: all P are Q; this is P; therefore this is Q.

questions.stimulus: In a midyear procurement review of a institutional program, the planning office concludes that the proposal should be adopted. The proposal would funded after-school music clubs. The office also says any proposal that would produce a measurable public benefit should be adopted.
questions.stem: Which one of the following, if assumed, enables the conclusion to be properly drawn?

choices.label: A

choices.text: No proposal should be adopted merely because it is inexpensive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The planning office has rejected proposals that lacked measurable public benefits.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A proposal can be adopted even if it has no measurable public benefit.

questions.explanation: The conclusion follows if the specific proposal falls within the class that the office says should be adopted.

choices.label: A

choices.label: B

choices.label: C

choices.label: D
choices.text: showing that the evidence offered for a causal claim is also compatible with a different explanation.

questions.stem: The reasoning in the argument is vulnerable because it

choices.label: B

choices.label: C
choices.text: treats a condition required for a status as though that condition were enough to guarantee the status.

choices.label: D

questions.stimulus: In a autumn operations review of a regional program, after the service change, the average wait time at Dalesford fell from 18 minutes to 11 minutes. The director concludes that every customer was served faster after the change.
